5.3.a.info:
The enable password command sets the password required to move from user EXEC mode (the > prompt) into privileged EXEC mode (the # prompt). It is one of the oldest Cisco IOS commands — and one of the most dangerous if used in production, because it stores the password as Type 0 (plaintext) in the running configuration.

The core flaw: anyone with read access to the config can see the password. show running-config, TFTP backups, emailed configs, screenshot shares — all expose the cleartext password. Even with service password-encryption enabled, the password is only upgraded to Type 7 (Vigenere) which is trivially reversible with free online decoders.

Key behavioral rules:

  • Stored as Type 0 by default in show running-config
  • When both enable password and enable secret exist, enable secret ALWAYS wins. The enable password value is effectively ignored.
  • Cannot be hashed natively — it only supports Type 0 or Type 7 (with service password-encryption)
  • Cisco marks it deprecated. Best practice is to remove it entirely and use only enable secret.

Exam trap: a show running-config snippet shows both enable password cisco and enable secret cisco123. Which password gets you into priv exec? Answer: cisco123 — because enable secret takes precedence.

hack.memory: enable PASSWORD = PLAIN to see. enable SECRET = Securely encrypted. Both configured? SECRET wins, password is ignored. Rule: if you can READ the password in show run, it is the wrong one.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure enable password cisco AND enable secret cisco123. Exit to user mode, try enable with 'cisco' (fails) then 'cisco123' (works). Then show run | include enable to see cisco in plaintext and cisco123 as a $1$ hash.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4 (CLI basics) + Day 52 (Device Security). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6 + 27. The exam loves to show both commands in the same config and ask which is enforced — always enable secret.

5.3.a.info:
enable secret [password] sets the privileged EXEC password and stores it as a one-way hash — by default Type 5 (MD5), or Type 8 (PBKDF2-SHA256) / Type 9 (scrypt) on newer IOS versions. Unlike enable password, the original password cannot be recovered from the config.

When you type enable secret MyPass123, IOS immediately hashes the password and stores only the hash string, which looks like enable secret 5 $1$mERr$hx5rVt7rPNoS4wqbXKX7m0. The 5 indicates Type 5 (MD5). An admin reviewing the config sees the hash but cannot reverse it back to MyPass123.

Precedence rule (must memorize): if both enable password and enable secret are configured on the same device, enable secret always takes precedence. The IOS authentication logic ignores enable password entirely when an enable secret exists.

Command variants:

  • enable secret MyPass — default Type 5 on older IOS, may default to Type 9 on newer IOS
  • enable algorithm-type scrypt secret MyPass — force Type 9 (strongest)
  • enable algorithm-type sha256 secret MyPass — force Type 8
  • enable secret 5 $1$abc$hashvalue — paste an already-hashed password (migration scenario)

hack.memory: enable SECRET = Securely hashed. Preferred. Cannot be reversed. Wins over enable password every time. Type 5 = $1$ hash prefix = MD5. Type 8 = PBKDF2. Type 9 = scrypt (strongest).
hack.practice: Lab: enable secret Cisco123!. Then show run | include enable secret. See the hash. Paste it into a Type 5 decoder online — it cannot be reversed (only brute-forced). Compare with enable password (instantly readable).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam expects you to know: enable secret uses hashing, always wins over enable password, Type 5 default (MD5) with newer IOS supporting Type 8/9.

5.3.a.info:
enable password [password] is the LEGACY method to protect privileged EXEC access. It stores the password as Type 0 (plaintext) in the running configuration. It exists only for backward compatibility with ancient IOS versions and should be considered deprecated in all modern deployments.

Why it is weak:

  • Type 0 = no encryption, no hashing — literally the ASCII text of the password in show run
  • Even when service password-encryption is applied, it upgrades only to Type 7 (Vigenere cipher with a known fixed key) — reversible in milliseconds with any online Cisco Type 7 decoder
  • Password is visible to anyone with read access to the config: backup server admins, version control, emailed configs, screen shares

Ignored when enable secret is set: if both commands are configured, IOS authentication completely ignores enable password. The only password that works is the one set by enable secret. Many admins mistakenly think the two must match — they don't. You can type enable password A and enable secret B — only B works.

Exam guidance: if a question asks what command to use to protect priv exec, the answer is enable secret, never enable password. If a config shows both, know that enable secret wins.

hack.memory: enable password = the password you can SEE. Legacy. Type 0 by default. Even Type 7 is reversible. If enable secret exists, this command is a DECOY — completely ignored.
hack.practice: Configure enable password cisco, run show run | i enable — see 'cisco' in plaintext. Add service password-encryption, run show run again — see Type 7 hash. Paste it into an online Type 7 decoder, get 'cisco' back instantly.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam tests the precedence rule (secret wins) and the Type 0/Type 7 weakness. Memorize: enable password = insecure legacy, always override with enable secret.

5.3.a.info:
This is the single most tested rule in 5.3: when both enable password and enable secret are configured, enable secret ALWAYS wins. The enable password value is parsed into the config but is never used for authentication.

Why this rule exists: Cisco introduced enable secret as the secure replacement for enable password. To ease migration, they kept both commands valid — but the authentication logic was updated so that IOS checks for enable secret first. If found, it is used exclusively. The legacy enable password becomes a zombie value in the config, still visible but never enforced.

Config example:

R1(config)# enable password cisco
R1(config)# enable secret ciscoSecure123
R1(config)# exit
R1# disable
R1> enable
Password: cisco         <-- rejected
Password: ciscoSecure123 <-- accepted

Classic exam trap: the question shows a config snippet with both commands and asks 'What password does the admin type at the Password: prompt?' Many students pick enable password because it was configured first (or was typed last, or because they assume both work). The correct answer is always the enable secret value.

Best practice: never configure enable password in modern deployments. If you inherit a device with both, remove enable password with no enable password to eliminate the confusion and the plaintext exposure in the config.

children.name: YES: use enable secret (ignore enable password)
NO: fall back to enable password

hack.memory: Both set? SECRET WINS. Every time. No exceptions. enable password becomes a ghost in the config — visible, parseable, but never used for authentication.
hack.practice: Build the config with both commands, different values. Try to log in with each. Only the secret works. Then no enable password to clean up. Teach yourself that the legacy command is just noise.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. 100% guaranteed exam concept — Cisco loves this trap. Know the precedence rule cold.

5.3.a.info:
Type 0 means the password is stored as literal ASCII text in the running configuration — no encryption, no hashing, no obfuscation. It is the default storage for enable password, line passwords (password cisco under line con 0), and username X password Y.

Identifying Type 0 in configs:

  • enable password 0 cisco — explicit Type 0
  • enable password cisco — implicit Type 0 (no digit means plaintext)
  • password cisco under a line — plaintext line password
  • username admin password cisco — plaintext local user password

Risks:

  • Any config backup exposes the password
  • Anyone with show running-config access sees the password
  • Passwords end up in monitoring tools, SIEM logs, ticket attachments, screenshots
  • Even brief accidental exposure (Slack paste, email, screen share) is a permanent compromise — the password cannot be 'un-seen'

Mitigations in order of strength:

  1. Type 7 via service password-encryption — weak obfuscation, reversible, better than nothing
  2. Type 5 via enable secret or username X secret Y — one-way MD5 hash
  3. Type 8 (PBKDF2-SHA256) or Type 9 (scrypt) — strong modern hashes, use when IOS supports them

Rule of thumb: any time you see a Type 0 password in a production config, it is a finding — fix it immediately.

hack.memory: Type 0 = zero protection. Plaintext. Readable by anyone with show-run access. Use the 'secret' keyword or enable service password-encryption to upgrade — but hashed (Type 5/8/9) beats encrypted (Type 7) every time.
hack.practice: Configure username test password MyPass. Run show run | i username — see 'MyPass' in plaintext. Now configure username test2 secret MyPass. Run show run again — see $1$ hash for test2. Proves secret > password.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Memorize the type ladder: 0 (plain), 7 (weak encrypt), 5 (MD5), 8 (PBKDF2), 9 (scrypt). Exam shows hashes and asks to identify the type — the $1$ prefix = Type 5.

5.3.b.term: enable secret and password hash types
5.3.b.info:
The enable secret command sets a hashed password for privileged EXEC mode. The hash type depends on the IOS version and the optional algorithm-type keyword. Understanding the five Cisco password types is a high-frequency exam topic.

The five password types (memorize cold):

  • Type 0 — Plaintext. No protection. Default for enable password and line password commands. Visible verbatim in show running-config.
  • Type 4 — SHA-256. Deprecated in later IOS because it used a single unsalted SHA-256 pass. Superseded by Type 8.
  • Type 5 — MD5 hash. Default for legacy enable secret. Recognizable by the $1$ prefix. One-way. MD5 is weak by modern standards but the hash still cannot be reversed — only brute-forced.
  • Type 7 — Vigenere cipher. Reversible. Applied only by service password-encryption. Weak obfuscation, not true encryption. Cracked in seconds by any Cisco Type 7 decoder.
  • Type 8 — PBKDF2 with SHA-256. Modern, strong. 20,000 iterations. Use on newer IOS when available.
  • Type 9 — scrypt. Strongest. Memory-hard — resists GPU/ASIC brute-force. Slowest to compute (intentional). Preferred on any IOS that supports it.

Forcing a specific type:

  • enable algorithm-type scrypt secret MyPass → Type 9
  • enable algorithm-type sha256 secret MyPass → Type 8
  • enable algorithm-type md5 secret MyPass → Type 5
  • enable secret MyPass → depends on IOS default (usually Type 5 on older, Type 9 on newer)

Precedence reminder: if both enable password and enable secret exist, enable secret always wins — regardless of the secret's hash type.

hack.memory: 0-4-5-7-8-9. Plain, SHA256-old, MD5, Vigenere, PBKDF2, scrypt. Only 5 matters for the default enable secret. Only 7 is 'encryption' (reversible). 8 and 9 are modern and strong. Rank: 9 > 8 > 5 > 7 > 0.
hack.practice: On a lab router: enable algorithm-type scrypt secret MyScrypt then enable algorithm-type md5 secret MyMD5 then enable secret MyDefault. Check show run | i enable secret — see the 9, 5, and default prefixes. Identify each.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4 + 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6 + 27. Exam asks 'which type is MD5?' (5), 'which is reversible?' (7), 'which is strongest?' (9). Drill these numbers until automatic.

5.3.b.info:
Type 0 means the password is stored verbatim in the configuration — no encryption, no hashing, no scrambling. It is the default for legacy commands like enable password, line password, and username X password Y.

How to spot it:

  • Literal text in show running-config: password cisco or enable password cisco
  • Explicit 0 indicator: password 0 cisco — the 0 means Type 0

Why it fails security audits: any time the config is viewed, copied, backed up, emailed, pasted into a ticket, or shown on a screen share, the password is instantly compromised. No after-the-fact mitigation exists — once seen, it is forever exposed.

Upgrading from Type 0:

  • Add service password-encryption globally to convert existing Type 0 passwords to Type 7 (weak but better than nothing)
  • Better: replace password with secret keyword in enable and username commands — this forces hashing (Type 5 or higher)

hack.memory: Type 0 = Zero protection. The number matches the reality. If you can read the password without a decoder, it is Type 0.
hack.practice: Set username test password Lab123. Run show run | i username — 'Lab123' is plainly visible. Delete and replace with username test secret Lab123. Run show run again — now it is a $1$ hash.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Exam identifies Type 0 via the plaintext string or explicit '0' indicator. Always avoid in production.

5.3.b.info:
Type 5 is the classic Cisco MD5 hash, used by enable secret by default on older IOS versions. The hash is one-way — IOS cannot reverse it to recover the original password. Authentication works by hashing the user's input and comparing the two hashes.

How to recognize Type 5:

  • In the config: enable secret 5 $1$mERr$hx5rVt7rPNoS4wqbXKX7m0
  • The 5 after 'secret' indicates the type
  • The hash string starts with $1$ — this is the Unix/Cisco MD5 identifier
  • Structure: $1$[salt]$[hash] — the salt prevents rainbow-table attacks

Strengths:

  • One-way — cannot be reversed mathematically
  • Salted — two admins with the same password get different hashes
  • Widely supported across all modern IOS versions

Weaknesses:

  • MD5 is cryptographically broken for collision resistance (not relevant to password hashing, but signals aging crypto)
  • Fast to compute — GPUs can brute-force billions of MD5 hashes per second against weak passwords
  • For short or dictionary-word passwords, Type 5 can be cracked in hours
  • Superseded by Type 8 (PBKDF2) and Type 9 (scrypt) on modern IOS

Bottom line: Type 5 is far better than Type 0 or Type 7, but if the platform supports it, use Type 8 or Type 9 instead.

hack.memory: Type 5 = MD5 = $1$ prefix. One-way, salted, but fast to brute-force. Better than 0 or 7. Worse than 8 or 9. Default for old enable secret.
hack.practice: Run enable secret Cisco123. Check show run | i enable secret. The line shows enable secret 5 $1$.... The '5' and '$1$' both confirm MD5.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam loves the $1$ prefix identifier — memorize it.

5.3.b.info:
Type 7 is a reversible Vigenere-cipher obfuscation applied by the service password-encryption global command. It is the weakest 'encryption' Cisco offers — intended only to stop casual shoulder-surfing, not actual attackers.

How it works:

  • Cisco uses a fixed, publicly known Vigenere key to XOR-obfuscate the plaintext password
  • The resulting hex string is stored with a 7 type indicator: password 7 0822455D0A16
  • Any Cisco Type 7 decoder (dozens of free websites, the Python module ciscot7, etc.) reverses the obfuscation instantly

What service password-encryption affects:

  • Line passwords (password under line con/vty/aux)
  • enable password (Type 0 → Type 7)
  • username X password Y entries
  • Does NOT affect any password set with the secret keyword — those remain Type 5/8/9

Important gotchas:

  • Running no service password-encryption does NOT un-encrypt passwords already converted — they stay Type 7 until manually rewritten
  • The same plaintext produces the same Type 7 hash every time — no salt, so two admins with 'cisco' have identical ciphertext
  • Type 7 is considered obfuscation, not encryption — Cisco documentation itself warns against relying on it for security

Use case: a safety net. Enable service password-encryption as a baseline so that if an admin accidentally sets a line password with password instead of using secret, at least it is not sitting in the config as Type 0.

hack.memory: Type 7 = Weak. Vigenere. Reversible. Like writing a secret in pig-latin — technically hidden, actually trivial. Use 'secret' keyword instead to get Type 5/8/9.
hack.practice: Configure line con 0 → password cisco → exit → service password-encryption. Run show run | i password — see password 7 0822455D0A16. Paste into ifm.net.nz/cookbooks/passwordcracker.html — instantly returns 'cisco'.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam tests: Type 7 is REVERSIBLE, applied by service password-encryption, weak. The '7' indicator and hex string are the visual giveaway.

5.3.b.info:
Type 8 uses PBKDF2 (Password-Based Key Derivation Function 2) with SHA-256. It is a modern, strong password-hashing algorithm introduced on newer IOS/IOS-XE versions.

How PBKDF2 works:

  • Takes the password + salt as input
  • Applies SHA-256 hashing repeatedly — Cisco uses 20,000 iterations by default
  • The iteration count deliberately slows down hashing, making brute-force attacks 20,000x more expensive than a single SHA-256 pass
  • Output is a long, salted hash that cannot be reversed

How to identify Type 8:

  • The config line shows enable secret 8 $8$salt$hashvalue
  • The 8 indicator follows 'secret'
  • Hash prefix $8$

Enabling Type 8:

  • enable algorithm-type sha256 secret MyPass → forces Type 8
  • username admin algorithm-type sha256 secret MyPass → local user with Type 8

When to use: on any IOS that supports Type 8 or Type 9, prefer these over Type 5. Type 8 is much stronger than MD5 against GPU-based brute-force. Type 9 (scrypt) is even stronger because it is memory-hard, but Type 8 is a solid modern choice.

A common deployment pattern: set enable algorithm-type scrypt secret for the enable password (Type 9), and use Type 8 for local user accounts if scrypt creates performance concerns at scale.

hack.memory: Type 8 = PBKDF2-SHA256. $8$ prefix. 20,000 iterations = slow by design. Strong modern hash. Use when IOS supports it.
hack.practice: On a newer IOS: enable algorithm-type sha256 secret LabPass. Verify with show run | i enable secret — expect enable secret 8 $8$.... Compare the hash length to Type 5 — Type 8 is much longer.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam knows Type 8 = PBKDF2, Type 9 = scrypt, and both are stronger than Type 5.

5.3.b.info:
Type 9 uses the scrypt key derivation function — the strongest password hash Cisco IOS currently offers. scrypt was designed specifically to resist the kind of hardware-accelerated brute-force attacks that threaten faster hashes like MD5 and even SHA-256.

Why scrypt is stronger:

  • Memory-hard — computing a single scrypt hash requires a significant amount of RAM, not just CPU cycles
  • GPUs and ASICs excel at parallel hashing with minimal memory per thread. scrypt forces each hash attempt to consume memory, neutralizing the GPU/ASIC advantage
  • An attacker with a high-end GPU farm that could crack billions of MD5 hashes per second may only manage thousands of scrypt hashes per second
  • Used by cryptocurrencies like Litecoin specifically for its resistance to specialized mining hardware

Identifying Type 9:

  • Config line: enable secret 9 $9$salt$hashvalue
  • The 9 indicator after 'secret'
  • Hash prefix $9$

Enabling Type 9:

  • enable algorithm-type scrypt secret MyPass → Type 9
  • username admin algorithm-type scrypt secret MyPass → local user with Type 9

Best practice: on any IOS that supports Type 9, use it for enable secret and high-value local accounts. The slight CPU/memory overhead during authentication is negligible compared to the security benefit.

hack.memory: Type 9 = scrypt = the STRONGEST. $9$ prefix. Memory-hard = breaks GPU farms. Top tier. Always preferred when IOS supports it.
hack.practice: On a newer IOS router: enable algorithm-type scrypt secret MyPass. Show run — confirm Type 9 and $9$ prefix. Compare hash length and format across Types 5, 8, 9 — each is distinct and recognizable.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam: 'strongest Cisco password hash?' = Type 9 (scrypt). 'Identifier prefix?' = $9$. Drill these.

5.3.c.info:
The console port is the physical RJ-45 or USB port on a Cisco router or switch used for direct, out-of-band management. It is accessed via a rollover cable from a laptop running a terminal emulator (PuTTY, SecureCRT, Tera Term). Console access is essential for initial configuration, password recovery, and emergency troubleshooting when network access is lost.

Securing the console requires TWO commands working together:

  • line console 0 — enter console-line configuration mode (there is only one console port, hence 0)
  • password [pw] — set the line password
  • login — CRITICAL: tells IOS to actually prompt for the password on connection. Without this, the password is set but never requested.
  • exec-timeout [min] [sec] — auto-disconnect idle sessions (default 10 minutes)

The 'login' gotcha: this is one of the most tested traps on the exam. Configuring password cisco alone does NOT secure the console — the login command is what enables password enforcement. Forgetting login leaves the console wide open despite the password being configured.

Better alternative — local authentication:

username admin secret MyPass123
line console 0
 login local
 exec-timeout 5 0

login local replaces the simple line password with the local user database. Each admin logs in with their own username, providing individual accountability in logs.

Additional useful commands:

  • logging synchronous — prevents log messages from breaking up commands you are typing
  • exec-timeout 0 0 — disables timeout (NOT recommended for security)

hack.memory: Console = direct PHYSICAL access port. line console 0 (there's only one). Set password + type 'login' or it is ignored. login local = use the username database instead of a shared password.
hack.practice: Lab: line con 0 → password cisco — deliberately OMIT login. Disconnect, reconnect via console — you get straight to EXEC with NO prompt. That is the gotcha. Add login, reconnect, now it prompts. Lesson learned permanently.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4 + 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6 + 27. The 'login' gotcha is a classic exam trap — password set but not enforced. Memorize: password + login = enforced.

5.3.c.info:
The canonical console-hardening sequence:

Router(config)# line console 0
Router(config-line)# password SecureConsole123
Router(config-line)# login
Router(config-line)# exec-timeout 5 0
Router(config-line)# logging synchronous
Router(config-line)# exit

Command-by-command:

  • line console 0 — the ONE console port. The 0 is literally the index (there is no console 1). Entering this mode changes the prompt to (config-line)#.
  • password [pw] — sets the simple line password. Stored as Type 0 by default, Type 7 if service password-encryption is enabled.
  • login — REQUIRED to enforce the password. Without this, IOS knows the password exists but will not prompt.
  • exec-timeout 5 0 — disconnect after 5 minutes of idle time. The two numbers are minutes and seconds.
  • logging synchronous — quality-of-life: when a syslog message interrupts your typing, IOS re-displays the command line so you can continue without retyping.

Alternative using local auth:

username admin secret Cisco123!
line console 0
 login local
 exec-timeout 5 0
 logging synchronous

login local is preferred in production — it provides per-user accountability and replaces the weak shared line password with a hashed entry in the local user database.

hack.memory: Four lines to harden the console: line con 0, password, login, exec-timeout. The middle two MUST be together — setting password without login = password never asked.
hack.practice: Memorize the block. Type it blindfolded in Packet Tracer 10 times until it is muscle memory. On exam simulations you will be asked to secure the console with an exact command sequence.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4. Exam tests exact command order and the login requirement.

5.3.c.info:
The #1 exam trap in console/VTY configuration: password [pw] alone does NOT enforce password prompts. The login command is what tells IOS to actually challenge incoming connections for the password.

What 'login' does:

  • login (plain) — prompts for the line password configured by password [pw]
  • login local — prompts for a username/password pair from the local user database (username X secret Y)
  • login authentication [AAA-method] — uses a named AAA method list (RADIUS/TACACS+)
  • no login — disables password prompts entirely (allows anonymous access IF no other auth method is present)

The trap scenario (word-for-word exam style):

line console 0
 password cisco

Question: 'Is the console secured?' Answer: NO. The password is configured but never prompted for — anyone with physical access walks straight to the EXEC prompt.

Fix:

line console 0
 password cisco
 login

Now the console prompts for 'cisco' on connection.

VTY version of the gotcha: VTY lines behave differently — if no password AND no login exists, IOS blocks all Telnet/SSH connections with 'Password required, but none set.' This is intentional: Cisco refuses passwordless remote access by default. So the gotcha manifests differently — on console, missing login = wide open; on VTY, missing login + missing password = connection refused.

hack.memory: password = the password. LOGIN = the command that makes IOS ASK. No login = no prompt = no security. Password without login is like installing a lock but never closing the door.
hack.practice: Lab the broken config in Packet Tracer. Reconnect — straight to > prompt, no password asked. Add login. Reconnect — now prompted. Seeing the unsecured behavior firsthand burns the lesson in.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. Exam tests this directly: 'why isn't the password being requested?' — answer is the missing login command.

5.3.c.info:
logging synchronous is a quality-of-life command configured under console, VTY, or AUX lines. It prevents log messages (syslog events like interface up/down, OSPF adjacency changes, etc.) from garbling commands that are being typed.

Problem without it:

Router# configure terminal
Router(config)# interface g
*Mar 1 00:05:23: %LINK-3-UPDOWN: Interface GigabitEthernet0/1, changed state to down
igabitEthernet0/0

A log message appeared mid-command. IOS doesn't re-display your input — you have to visually reconstruct what you were typing. On a busy router with frequent syslog events, this is maddening.

With logging synchronous: when IOS needs to output a log message while you are typing, it prints the log, then re-displays your current command line on a new prompt, so you can see exactly what you were typing and continue seamlessly.

Configuration:

line console 0
 logging synchronous
line vty 0 15
 logging synchronous

Security angle: not a security feature per se, but it is listed in 5.3 because it is part of the standard console-hardening block. Most engineers add it reflexively when configuring any management line.

Levels option: logging synchronous level [0-7] — only messages at or above this severity trigger the re-display. Default is level 2 (critical).

left.label: Without sync
items: Hard to use in busy networks

hack.memory: logging SYNCHRONOUS = keep the log and the CLI in sync. Your typing never gets eaten by a syslog message. Quality of life, not security.
hack.practice: In a busy lab, bring interfaces up/down repeatedly while typing long commands. Without logging synchronous, your commands get cut off by up/down messages. With it, the command reappears after each log.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4. Low weight but mentioned in CCNA context. Standard part of line-configuration best-practice blocks.

5.3.c.info:
exec-timeout [minutes] [seconds] automatically logs out an idle console, VTY, or AUX session after the specified inactivity period. It is a baseline security control — if an admin walks away from a logged-in session, the session disconnects on its own.

Defaults and ranges:

  • Default: 10 minutes, 0 seconds
  • Range: 0 minutes 0 seconds (disabled) to 35791 minutes 2147483 seconds (essentially forever)
  • exec-timeout 0 0 = NO timeout — session stays logged in indefinitely (DO NOT use in production)

Recommended values:

  • Console: 5-10 minutes (admins are usually physically present)
  • VTY (remote): 5 minutes — tighter because remote sessions are more exposed
  • AUX: 5 minutes if used; no exec if not used

Configuration:

line console 0
 exec-timeout 5 0
line vty 0 15
 exec-timeout 5 0

Security rationale: an admin leaves a session open, walks to lunch, and an attacker (or an unauthorized colleague) walks up and starts issuing privileged commands. exec-timeout limits this window — the session disconnects after 5 minutes of idle time, requiring re-authentication.

Gotcha: long-running commands (like debug or show tech-support) do count as activity, so they won't trigger timeout. But if you are reading a large show output and pause scrolling, you can time out. Mitigate with terminal length 0 to dump without paging, or just move quickly.

hack.memory: exec-timeout X Y = minutes SPACE seconds. Default 10 min. 'exec-timeout 0 0' = NEVER timeout = bad. Recommended: 5 min on all management lines.
hack.practice: Lab: line con 0 → exec-timeout 0 30 (30 sec). Log in, wait 30 sec without typing — session disconnects. Critical security control, cheap to configure.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4 + 52. Security best practice on every management line. Exam may ask the syntax — it's minutes first, then seconds.

5.3.d.info:
VTY (Virtual TeleType) lines are logical management interfaces used for remote administrative access via Telnet or SSH. They are not physical ports — they are software constructs that IOS creates to allow multiple simultaneous remote sessions. Most Cisco devices support 16 VTY lines (0-15), meaning up to 16 admins can SSH into the device at the same time.

Essential VTY hardening:

username admin secret Cisco123!
line vty 0 15
 login local
 transport input ssh
 access-class 10 in
 exec-timeout 5 0
ip access-list standard 10
 permit 192.168.100.0 0.0.0.255

Command breakdown:

  • line vty 0 15 — configure all 16 VTY lines at once (range syntax)
  • login local — authenticate against the local username database. Preferred over simple login + line password.
  • transport input ssh — allow ONLY SSH. Block Telnet (which sends passwords in cleartext). Other options: telnet, all, none, ssh telnet.
  • access-class [ACL] in — apply an ACL to restrict WHICH source IPs can even attempt to connect. Note: access-class (VTY) is different from access-group (interfaces).
  • exec-timeout 5 0 — disconnect idle sessions after 5 minutes

The 'no password, no access' rule: if a VTY line has NO password configured and NO login local/AAA method, IOS refuses all incoming connections with the message 'Password required, but none set.' This is a safety default — Cisco will not allow passwordless remote access.

SSH prerequisites: for transport input ssh to actually work, you also need:

  1. A hostname (hostname MyRouter)
  2. A domain name (ip domain-name lab.local)
  3. An RSA key pair (crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048)
  4. SSH version 2 (ip ssh version 2)
  5. At least one local user (username X secret Y)

steps: <- Access granted or denied by access-class

hack.memory: VTY = Virtual TeletYpe = remote lines (Telnet/SSH). 16 lines (0-15). No password = connection refused. access-CLASS restricts by IP (not access-group). transport input SSH blocks Telnet.
hack.practice: Full hardening lab: username + line vty 0 15 + login local + transport input ssh + access-class 10 in + exec-timeout. From a permitted IP, SSH works. From a non-permitted IP, connection refused. Telnet always refused.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4 + 43 (SSH) + 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6 + 27. High-frequency exam topic. Know the 5-command hardening block cold.

5.3.d.info:
Two valid approaches to authenticate VTY access:

1) Simple line password (basic, not preferred):

line vty 0 15
 password RemotePass123
 login
 transport input ssh
 exec-timeout 5 0

Every admin uses the same shared password. No individual accountability. The login command is required — without it, the password is configured but not enforced (same gotcha as console).

2) Local authentication (preferred):

username admin privilege 15 secret Cisco123!
username helpdesk privilege 1 secret HelpDesk456
line vty 0 15
 login local
 transport input ssh
 exec-timeout 5 0

Each admin has their own credentials. login local references the local user database. Syslog and audit trails show WHO connected, enabling accountability.

3) AAA with RADIUS/TACACS+ (enterprise, covered in 5.8):

aaa new-model
aaa authentication login default group radius local
line vty 0 15
 login authentication default

Credentials validated against a central RADIUS or TACACS+ server. Scalable for hundreds of devices.

Configuring a range of lines: line vty 0 15 configures all 16 lines simultaneously. On older IOS or smaller platforms you may see only 5 lines (line vty 0 4). Always configure the FULL range to avoid having some hardened lines and some unsecured lines.

hack.memory: Three VTY auth models: shared password + login, login local (usernames), AAA. For CCNA basics: use login local. Always configure line vty 0 15 (all 16) — don't leave some unsecured.
hack.practice: Build the 5-command VTY hardening block. Test with Telnet (should fail — transport input ssh). Test with SSH from allowed IP (works). Test from unauthorized IP (access-class blocks). Verify with show users.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4 + 43. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6. Exam tests the login-local pattern heavily.

5.3.d.info:
Cisco IOS refuses all remote logins on a VTY line that has no password AND no alternate authentication method configured. This is a built-in safety feature — Cisco will not allow passwordless SSH or Telnet access by default.

What triggers the refusal:

  • VTY line has no password configured
  • AND no login local (no local user database reference)
  • AND no AAA method list applied

Result: incoming connection receives the error:

% Password required, but none set.

The connection is immediately terminated by the device.

How to resolve: configure at least one authentication method:

  1. password [pw] + login — shared line password
  2. username X secret Y + login local — local user database (preferred)
  3. AAA method list + login authentication — RADIUS/TACACS+

Why this default matters: contrast with the console port — if you don't configure a password on console, you still get in (console is trusted because it requires physical access). On VTY (remote access), Cisco assumes the network is hostile and refuses by default. This protects against admins accidentally exposing a new device.

Exam trap: 'User cannot SSH to the router despite SSH being enabled globally. Why?' Check the VTY lines — if password/login-local is missing, that's the answer.

hack.memory: VTY with no auth = connection REFUSED. Cisco default. 'Password required, but none set' is the dead giveaway message. Console = open without pw; VTY = closed without pw.
hack.practice: Lab: on a fresh VTY line, enable SSH globally but do NOT configure a password or login local. SSH in — get the error. Add login local with a username — SSH now works.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43. Exam may show this exact error message as a troubleshooting clue.

5.3.d.info:
access-class [ACL-number | ACL-name] in applies an access control list to VTY lines to restrict which source IP addresses are allowed to connect. This is critical for production security — even with strong passwords, limiting SSH access to a small management subnet dramatically reduces attack surface.

Configuration example:

ip access-list standard MGMT_ONLY
 permit 192.168.100.0 0.0.0.255
 permit host 10.1.1.50
 deny any
!
line vty 0 15
 access-class MGMT_ONLY in
 login local
 transport input ssh

Only hosts in 192.168.100.0/24 or the specific host 10.1.1.50 can even attempt to SSH. All other source IPs are silently dropped at the VTY layer before authentication is even attempted.

Key behaviors:

  • in = inbound (connections TO the device). out = outbound (connections FROM the device's VTY to other devices)
  • Works with both standard and extended ACLs, and named or numbered ACLs
  • Silent drop — unauthorized hosts get connection timeout, not an error message. Attackers cannot tell if the device exists.
  • Applied per-line — you can configure different access-classes on different VTY line ranges if needed, though typically all 16 lines get the same policy

Best practice: always restrict VTY to a dedicated management subnet. Combine with password/login-local for defense in depth. This is one of the highest-ROI security controls on any network device.

hack.memory: access-CLASS = VTY. access-GROUP = interface. Never confuse them. Use access-class to restrict SSH to a management subnet. Silent drop means attackers don't even get a login prompt.
hack.practice: Lab: ACL permitting only your PC's IP, applied with access-class 10 in. SSH from your PC — works. Change your PC's IP outside the permit range — SSH times out with no response. That's the silent drop.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43 + 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6 + 27. High exam weight. Critical real-world control.

5.3.d.info:
Two similar-sounding commands, two entirely different purposes. Confusing them is a common exam pitfall.

access-class [ACL] in|out

  • Applied under line configuration (VTY lines)
  • Filters Telnet/SSH connections to/from the device itself
  • 'Who can remotely manage this device?'
  • Command context: line vty 0 15 → access-class 10 in

ip access-group [ACL] in|out

  • Applied under interface configuration (physical or logical interface)
  • Filters traffic PASSING THROUGH the interface
  • 'What traffic is allowed to traverse this interface?'
  • Command context: interface Gi0/0 → ip access-group 110 in

Memory device:

  • access-CLASS = line — 'which CLASS of users can access the management plane?'
  • access-GROUP = interface — 'which GROUP of packets can pass through this door?'

Exam scenario: 'You want to restrict SSH access to management subnet 192.168.100.0/24. Where is the ACL applied?' Answer: VTY lines with access-class, not the interface with ip access-group. Using access-group on the interface would also block legitimate transit traffic — completely different behavior.

Both can coexist: a router typically has both — access-class on VTY for management-plane protection, access-group on interfaces for transit traffic filtering. They don't conflict.

hack.memory: CLASS = line (management). GROUP = interface (transit). VTY gets access-class. Ethernet gets access-group. Mix them up and you configure the wrong control in the wrong place.
hack.practice: Build both: access-class 10 in on VTY to restrict SSH sources, AND ip access-group 110 in on Gi0/0 to block specific transit traffic. Test each independently. Understand they solve different problems.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 43 + 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6 + 27. Classic exam trap — Cisco loves to substitute one for the other in questions.

5.3.e.info:
The AUX (Auxiliary) port is a legacy physical port on Cisco routers designed for out-of-band management via an external modem. The original use case: when the production network is down and an admin needs to dial into the router over a POTS (Plain Old Telephone Service) line to fix it. An RJ-45 port on the router connects to a modem; the modem connects to a phone line; the admin dials from a remote site and authenticates.

In modern networks the AUX port is rarely used — cellular LTE out-of-band management and dedicated management networks have replaced dial-in. However, the port still exists on most Cisco routers, and it still needs to be secured or disabled, because an attacker with physical access could plug a modem or terminal into it.

Securing the AUX port (if in use):

line aux 0
 password AuxPass123
 login
 exec-timeout 5 0
 transport input none

transport input none ensures no network-based protocols (Telnet/SSH) can originate from a session on the AUX line.

Best practice — disable if not in use:

line aux 0
 no exec
 no password
 transport input none

no exec is the key command — it prevents any EXEC session from being established through the port. Even if someone plugs in a modem or terminal, they cannot get a CLI prompt.

Switches and the AUX port: most Cisco switches do NOT have an AUX port. Only routers have them. On a switch, you'll see line console 0 and line vty 0 15 but no line aux 0.

hack.memory: AUX = AUXiliary = the backup phone line for modem dial-in. Legacy. On routers only (not switches). Best practice if unused: 'no exec' to disable EXEC sessions entirely.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: line aux 0 → no exec. Run show line — confirms aux is disabled. Compare with show line con 0 and show line vty 0 15 to see the three management lines side by side.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Low exam weight — but Cisco may ask 'which command disables the AUX port?' = no exec.

5.3.e.info:
The AUX port on Cisco routers is an RJ-45 connector typically located next to the console port, often with an 'AUX' label silkscreened next to it. Internally it is a second asynchronous serial interface — IOS configures it via line aux 0.

Historical context: before dedicated management networks and cellular out-of-band (OOB) solutions, admins used the AUX port for emergency access. A modem connected to AUX, connected to a phone line. When the production network failed, the admin dialed the modem from home and got a CLI prompt — even though the primary network was dead.

Modern replacements:

  • Dedicated OOB management networks (separate switches, separate VLANs)
  • Cellular LTE OOB dongles (4G/5G backup management)
  • Console servers (Opengear, Lantronix) with VPN connectivity
  • iLO/iDRAC-style BMC (baseboard management controllers) on newer platforms

Why it still exists: backward compatibility. Many customers have deployed devices with AUX-based OOB that still works. Cisco leaves the port for legacy support.

Security consideration: even if unused, the AUX port is still a physical attack surface. A malicious insider or someone with physical access could plug in a laptop/terminal and attempt CLI access. Best practice: either disable with no exec OR secure it exactly like the console (password + login + exec-timeout).

hack.memory: AUX = auxiliary = modem dial-in. Like the fax line for routers. Rarely used today but still a physical port that needs hardening.
hack.practice: Identify the AUX port on a real router photo. Note: switches don't have one. Understand why it's a security concern even when unused.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Low-frequency topic.

5.3.e.info:
no exec is the command applied under line aux 0 to disable EXEC sessions on the AUX port entirely. After this command, even if someone connects a cable to the AUX port, IOS will not spawn a CLI session for them.

Configuration:

Router(config)# line aux 0
Router(config-line)# no exec
Router(config-line)# no password
Router(config-line)# transport input none
Router(config-line)# transport output none

Why this is the preferred approach:

  • AUX is rarely used in modern networks, so disabling it eliminates attack surface
  • Even if an attacker plugs in a modem or terminal adapter, no EXEC means no CLI access
  • Simpler than maintaining passwords on an unused port

Comparison with disabling VTY: you can do the same on unused VTY lines. If you only expect to use VTY 0-4 but still have VTY 5-15 available, you can apply transport input none on the unused range to block Telnet/SSH attempts there.

Verification:

Router# show line aux 0
   Tty Typ     Tx/Rx    A Modem  Roty AccO AccI  Uses   Noise  Overruns   Int
   65  AUX     9600/9600 -  -      -    -    -      0       0     0/0       -

The output shows the line is defined but if you configured no exec, attempting to connect via a modem/terminal results in no interactive session.

hack.memory: 'no exec' = the door is welded shut. Cable plugged in? Doesn't matter. No EXEC = no CLI. Best practice for unused AUX.
hack.practice: Lab: line aux 0 → no exec. Try to simulate AUX access — blocked. Good security hygiene for production devices.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Specific command exam may test — 'which command disables AUX EXEC?' = no exec.

5.3.f.info:
Local user authentication creates per-user username/password pairs stored directly in the device's running configuration. It is the preferred method for management access on small networks — each admin authenticates with their own credentials, enabling individual accountability in logs.

Creating a local user:

Router(config)# username admin privilege 15 secret Cisco123!
Router(config)# username helpdesk privilege 1 secret HelpDesk456
Router(config)# username readonly privilege 1 secret ReadOnly789

Critical: use secret not password:

  • username admin secret MyPass → Type 5/8/9 hash, one-way
  • username admin password MyPass → Type 0 (plaintext) unless service password-encryption → Type 7 (reversible)
  • Always use secret for new entries — same pattern as enable secret

Activating local auth on lines:

line console 0
 login local
line vty 0 15
 login local
 transport input ssh

The login local command tells IOS to authenticate against the local user database instead of a line password. Without login local, the usernames exist but are never checked.

Privilege levels 0-15: each user can be assigned a privilege level that controls what commands they can execute. Default is level 1 (user EXEC, show commands only). Level 15 is full privileged EXEC. Levels 2-14 are customizable.

Immediate enable-mode login: configuring a user with privilege 15 causes them to skip straight to # prompt on login — no enable command needed. This is standard for admin accounts.

When local auth is insufficient: at scale (more than ~20 devices), maintaining local user databases becomes unmanageable. For enterprise deployments, migrate to AAA with RADIUS or TACACS+ (covered in 5.8) for centralized user management.

children.name: login local applied to console + VTY

hack.memory: Local users = per-person accounts stored ON the device. Always use 'secret' (hashed), not 'password' (plaintext). Activate with 'login local' under each line — without it, usernames are ignored.
hack.practice: Lab: create three users at different privilege levels. Apply login local on console and VTY. Test each — admin gets # immediately, helpdesk gets > and cannot configure terminal.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam expects username X secret Y + login local pattern.

5.3.f.info:
username [name] secret [password] creates a local user with a hashed password in the running configuration. This is the preferred syntax — Cisco's modern recommendation — because the password is stored as a one-way hash (Type 5 MD5 by default, Type 8/9 on newer IOS).

Syntax variations:

  • username admin secret Cisco123 — basic, default hash type
  • username admin privilege 15 secret Cisco123 — with explicit privilege level
  • username admin algorithm-type scrypt secret Cisco123 — force Type 9 (scrypt)
  • username admin algorithm-type sha256 secret Cisco123 — force Type 8 (PBKDF2)
  • username admin secret 5 $1$salt$hashvalue — paste a pre-hashed password (migration)

Avoid the legacy password variant:

username admin password Cisco123    <-- Type 0 plaintext, WEAK

The password keyword stores the value as Type 0 (plaintext). Even with service password-encryption it upgrades only to Type 7 (reversible). Never use this for new user entries.

Viewing stored entries:

Router# show running-config | include username
username admin privilege 15 secret 5 $1$mERr$hx5rVt7rPNoS4wqbXKX7m0
username helpdesk privilege 1 secret 5 $1$abcde$fghij...

The hashes are visible but not reversible to the original passwords.

Deleting a user: no username [name] removes the entry entirely.

hack.memory: username X SECRET Y = hashed = safe. username X PASSWORD Y = plaintext = bad. Same rule as enable: always use 'secret'.
hack.practice: Create users with both syntaxes. show run | include username to see Type 5 hash for secret vs plaintext (or Type 7) for password. Proves the difference instantly.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Exam always tests secret vs password distinction.

5.3.f.info:
login local is the line-configuration command that tells IOS to authenticate incoming connections against the local user database (the username X secret Y entries in the running configuration), rather than a simple shared line password.

Where it applies:

  • line console 0 — console port authentication
  • line vty 0 15 — Telnet/SSH authentication
  • line aux 0 — AUX port (if used)

Effect: when a user connects, IOS prompts for a username AND password (not just a password). The username must match an entry from username X secret Y; the password is hashed and compared to the stored hash. A successful match logs the user in with their configured privilege level.

Without login local: even if usernames are configured with username X secret Y, IOS never checks them. Instead, it uses whatever authentication is configured on the line (simple line password + login, or nothing if neither is set).

Exam trap:

username admin secret Cisco123
line vty 0 15
 password RemotePass
 login

Which password works for SSH? Answer: RemotePass (the line password), because login alone uses the line password — NOT login local. Change to login local and now admin/Cisco123 is required instead.

Logs and accountability: with login local, IOS logs which username connected. show users displays active sessions with usernames, not just line numbers. Without it, you only see 'some admin connected on VTY 0' — no individual accountability.

hack.memory: login = use line password. login LOCAL = use LOCAL user database. Different commands, very different behavior. Without login local, your usernames sit unused.
hack.practice: Configure both usernames AND a line password. Test with just login — line password works, usernames ignored. Change to login local — usernames work, line password ignored. Exam trap clarified.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Essential exam concept.

5.3.f.info:
Cisco IOS supports privilege levels 0 through 15, with each level granting access to a specific set of commands. Assigning a privilege level to a user controls what that user can do after logging in.

The three built-in levels:

  • Level 0 — minimal access. Only 5 commands: logout, enable, disable, help, exit
  • Level 1 — default user EXEC level. show commands, ping, traceroute, but NO configure terminal, NO debug. The > prompt.
  • Level 15 — full privileged EXEC. All commands including configure terminal, show running-config, debug, erase. The # prompt.
  • Levels 2-14 — customizable intermediate levels. Use privilege exec level [N] [command] to assign specific commands to specific levels.

Configuring a priv-15 admin:

username admin privilege 15 secret Cisco123!

This user skips user EXEC entirely — on login they land directly at the # prompt with full access. No enable command required.

Example custom privilege:

privilege exec level 5 configure terminal
privilege exec level 5 show running-config
username networkops privilege 5 secret OpsPass123

Now the 'networkops' user at level 5 can run configure terminal and show running-config but cannot access commands reserved for levels 6-15.

Real-world usage: most enterprises use just two levels — privilege 1 for read-only helpdesk accounts and privilege 15 for full admins. Granular level customization is powerful but complex to manage; many sites delegate granularity to AAA servers (TACACS+ command authorization) instead of IOS privilege levels.

hack.memory: Privilege 0 = nothing. Priv 1 = look but don't touch. Priv 15 = god mode. Priv 15 users skip straight to # on login. Customize levels 2-14 with 'privilege exec level N command'.
hack.practice: Create a priv-1 user and a priv-15 user. Log in as each. Priv 1 gets >, cannot configure. Priv 15 gets #, full access. Try custom: assign configure terminal to level 5, test with a priv-5 user.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam tests the defaults (0, 1, 15) and the priv-15 auto-login behavior.

5.3.g.info:
The service password-encryption global configuration command applies a weak Vigenere-based Type 7 obfuscation to all plaintext (Type 0) passwords currently in the running configuration, and to any plaintext passwords configured afterward. It is a baseline control — better than nothing — but it is NOT real encryption and should never be relied upon for security.

What it does:

  • Converts enable password cisco from Type 0 to enable password 7 0822455D0A16
  • Converts line passwords (password cisco under console/VTY/AUX) to Type 7
  • Converts username X password Y to Type 7
  • Applies retroactively — existing Type 0 passwords in the config get re-encoded immediately upon enabling the command

What it does NOT do:

  • Does NOT affect enable secret (already Type 5/8/9)
  • Does NOT affect username X secret Y (already hashed)
  • Does NOT provide real encryption — Type 7 is reversible in seconds with any online Cisco Type 7 decoder or the ciscot7 Python module
  • Does NOT un-encrypt passwords if you run no service password-encryption — once converted, passwords remain Type 7 (but new plaintext passwords will not be re-encoded)

Proper use case: a safety net. Enable it globally on every device so that if someone accidentally sets a line password with the password keyword, it at least ends up as Type 7 instead of Type 0. But the real security comes from using secret keywords everywhere and from strong passwords.

Analogy: service password-encryption is like putting tissue paper over a window — it blocks the most casual glance but stops no one who actually wants to see through.

hack.memory: service password-encryption = weak obfuscation (Type 7). Reversible in SECONDS. Enable as a safety net, never as the primary control. Real security = 'secret' keyword = Type 5/8/9 hashing.
hack.practice: Lab: line con 0 → password cisco. Run show run — see 'cisco' plaintext. Then service password-encryption → show run — see Type 7 hex. Paste the hex into ifm.net.nz/cookbooks/passwordcracker.html — get 'cisco' back.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 6 + 27. High-weight exam topic — know what it encrypts, what it doesn't, and that Type 7 is trivially reversible.

5.3.g.info:
service password-encryption is a single-line global configuration command. When enabled, IOS automatically converts all existing Type 0 passwords in the running configuration to Type 7 (Vigenere-encoded hex strings), and encodes any future plaintext passwords the same way.

Commands:

Router(config)# service password-encryption        <-- enable
Router(config)# no service password-encryption     <-- disable (but existing Type 7 stays)

Immediate effect after enabling:

# Before
enable password cisco
!
line con 0
 password ConsolePass
!
username test password UserPass

# After 'service password-encryption'
enable password 7 0822455D0A16
!
line con 0
 password 7 02050D480809
!
username test password 7 13061E010803

Scope — what it covers:

  • All password keyword usages (line passwords, enable password, username password)
  • Applies retroactively to existing Type 0 entries
  • Covers BGP neighbor passwords, certain tunnel passwords, and other places where the password keyword appears

Scope — what it does NOT cover:

  • enable secret — stays as Type 5/8/9 hash
  • username X secret Y — stays as Type 5/8/9 hash
  • Any command that uses the secret keyword

One-way conversion: running no service password-encryption only affects future passwords. Existing passwords remain Type 7 until someone manually retypes them with the password command while pw-encryption is disabled.

hack.memory: One command, global effect: service password-encryption. Converts all existing AND future Type 0 passwords to Type 7. Doesn't touch 'secret' entries. Can't un-convert after the fact.
hack.practice: Enable on a lab router. show run before and after — observe all 'password' lines changing to Type 7. Try 'no service password-encryption' — existing ones stay Type 7 (one-way).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Exam tests the command syntax and the exact behavior — retroactive, applies to 'password' keyword only.

5.3.g.info:
Type 7 'encryption' is a Vigenere cipher with a fixed, publicly known key. It is reversible — the same plaintext produces the same Type 7 output, and any Type 7 string can be decoded back to plaintext in milliseconds.

Technical details:

  • The algorithm XORs each plaintext character against a fixed Vigenere key baked into IOS
  • The key is well-known — published in multiple security references and hardcoded into open-source decoders
  • The first two characters of the Type 7 output indicate the position offset in the Vigenere key (essentially a 'salt' — but predictable)
  • No cryptographic strength — this was always meant to stop casual config viewing, never a motivated attacker

Decoding tools (demonstrating weakness):

  • Online: ifm.net.nz/cookbooks/passwordcracker.html, packetlife.net/toolbox/type7 — paste hex, get plaintext
  • Python: pip install ciscot7 → ciscot7 -d '0822455D0A16' returns the plaintext
  • Kali Linux: hashcat with mode 7000 (older) or scripts like cisco-decrypt.c

Exam framing:

  • 'Which password type is REVERSIBLE?' → Type 7
  • 'What command generates Type 7 passwords?' → service password-encryption
  • 'Is Type 7 secure?' → NO, it is obfuscation, not encryption

Best practice mantra: if security matters, use secret (Type 5/8/9). Type 7 is a fallback only for situations where plaintext is the alternative.

hack.memory: Type 7 = 'Weakest type in the encryption sense.' Vigenere. Reversible. Cracked instantly online. Only good for stopping a shoulder-surf glance.
hack.practice: Install ciscot7 via pip. Take any password 7 [hex] from a lab config and run ciscot7 -d '[hex]'. Plaintext returns. Proves the algorithm is broken by design.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Exam: 'Which Cisco password type is reversible?' = 7. 'Applied by which command?' = service password-encryption.

5.3.g.info:
The guiding rule for every password on a Cisco device: use the secret keyword, never password. This single rule avoids every Type 0/Type 7 weakness and ensures passwords are stored as irreversible hashes.

Command pairs to memorize:

AVOIDUSE INSTEAD
enable password ciscoenable secret cisco
username admin password ciscousername admin secret cisco

Why secret always wins:

  • Stored as Type 5 (MD5) by default on older IOS — one-way hash
  • Stored as Type 9 (scrypt) by default on newer IOS — strongest
  • Can force Type 8 or 9 with algorithm-type keyword
  • Even with service password-encryption enabled, 'secret' entries remain hashed — not downgraded to Type 7

Exception — line passwords: line configuration (line con 0, line vty 0 15) does NOT support the secret keyword directly for line passwords. The workaround: use login local and store actual credentials via username X secret Y. Avoid simple line passwords entirely.

Combined best-practice block:

enable algorithm-type scrypt secret EnableSecretPass123!
username admin privilege 15 algorithm-type scrypt secret AdminSecretPass456!
service password-encryption
line console 0
 login local
 exec-timeout 5 0
line vty 0 15
 login local
 transport input ssh
 exec-timeout 5 0
 access-class MGMT in

Every password is Type 9 hashed. service password-encryption is a safety net for any stray plaintext. Access is restricted to SSH from a management subnet. This is the target state for a hardened Cisco device.

hack.memory: SECRET = hashed. PASSWORD = plaintext or weak. Rule: never type 'password' in a security command, always type 'secret'. If the command doesn't accept 'secret', use 'login local' with username secrets instead.
hack.practice: Audit a lab config. Grep for any 'password' lines. Replace each with 'secret' equivalents. Verify show run no longer has any Type 0 or Type 7 entries.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. The single most important security-command mantra. Exam rewards 'secret' over 'password' every time.

5.3.h.info:
Cisco IOS supports three types of banners that display messages to users connecting to a device. Banners serve legal, informational, and security purposes. The CCNA tests their syntax AND their display order.

The three banner types in display order:

  1. MOTD (Message of the Day) — banner motd # [text] # — displayed first, before any login prompt, to ALL connections. Used for legal disclaimers warning that unauthorized access is prohibited. This creates legal standing for prosecution.
  2. Login banner — banner login # [text] # — displayed after MOTD, before authentication. Used for additional pre-login notices (e.g., 'Enter your corporate credentials').
  3. EXEC banner — banner exec # [text] # — displayed after successful authentication. Used for post-login information (e.g., 'Maintenance window Sunday 2AM', 'Ticket queue at 12').

Display sequence on connection:

[MOTD banner — ALWAYS first]
[Login banner — before auth prompt]
Username: admin
Password: ******
[EXEC banner — after successful login]
Router#

Delimiter character: the # in the syntax is a delimiter — ANY character that does not appear in the banner text can be used. Common choices: #, $, ^. The delimiter marks where the banner text begins and ends — the banner continues across multiple lines until the closing delimiter is typed.

Content rules (heavily tested):

  • DO: Include a legal warning in MOTD: 'Authorized users only. All activity is monitored and logged. Unauthorized access is prohibited and will be prosecuted.'
  • DO NOT: Include the word 'Welcome' — a lawyer could argue it implies invitation, weakening prosecution
  • DO NOT: Include the hostname, IP address, device model, IOS version, location, or organization name — this gives attackers reconnaissance intel

hack.memory: Order: M-L-E. My Lawyer Explains. MOTD → Login → (auth) → EXEC. MOTD = legal warning. Login = pre-auth notice. EXEC = post-auth. NEVER say 'Welcome'. NEVER reveal device info.
hack.practice: Configure all three banners on a lab router. Reconnect and observe the exact order: MOTD first, login banner, auth prompt, then EXEC. Then study legal-notice banner templates from nist.gov or sans.org — use one in production.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam tests display order and content rules. 'Welcome to router R1' on MOTD = WRONG answer. A generic legal notice = correct.

5.3.h.info:
The banner display order is a high-frequency exam question. Memorize it cold: MOTD → Login → EXEC.

Full connection sequence:

  1. User initiates connection (console cable / SSH / Telnet)
  2. Device displays MOTD banner (if configured)
  3. Device displays Login banner (if configured)
  4. Device displays username/password prompt
  5. User authenticates successfully
  6. Device displays EXEC banner (if configured)
  7. User lands at CLI prompt (> or #)

What displays when auth fails: only MOTD and Login banners — EXEC banner is reserved for successful logins. This is intentional: an attacker attempting to guess passwords should see the legal warning (MOTD) but not see any post-auth informational content.

Mnemonic: My Lawyer Explains — MOTD, Login, EXEC. The lawyer analogy fits: MOTD is the legal warning you see before you even start.

Alternate mnemonic: 'Before, During, After' — MOTD is before everything, Login is during the login process (before auth), EXEC is after successful auth.

Exam scenario: 'An admin connects to the router via SSH. Which banner displays first?' Answer: MOTD (always first). If MOTD is not configured, the Login banner displays first. If neither is configured, the user goes straight to the auth prompt.

hack.memory: M-L-E order. MOTD first (always). Login second (pre-auth). EXEC last (post-auth). Fail auth = you see MOTD and Login but not EXEC. That's the safety feature.
hack.practice: Configure all three banners with obviously different text ('MOTD HERE', 'LOGIN HERE', 'EXEC HERE'). Connect. Observe the exact printing order. Fail an auth attempt — note EXEC never appears.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Exam frequently tests 'which banner appears first?' = MOTD.

5.3.h.info:
The MOTD banner is the most legally important banner. Its content should be carefully crafted to support potential prosecution while avoiding common mistakes.

REQUIRED content:

  • Clear statement that access is restricted to authorized users
  • Explicit warning that unauthorized access is prohibited
  • Notice that all activity is monitored and/or logged
  • Reference to applicable laws (e.g., 'violators will be prosecuted to the fullest extent of the law')

PROHIBITED content:

  • The word 'Welcome' — legal argument: 'Welcome' is an invitation, which undermines the claim that the attacker knew they were unauthorized. Courts have ruled in favor of defendants because banners said 'Welcome to [company] router.'
  • Hostname, device model, IOS version — gives attackers targeted reconnaissance. An attacker who sees 'Cisco ISR 4321, IOS 15.7(3)M2' knows exactly which CVEs to look up.
  • IP address, location, rack number — physical/network intelligence
  • Organization name or department — helps attackers craft social engineering attacks
  • Contact info (phone, email) — attackers can use this for phishing

Good example (use this style):

WARNING: This system is for authorized use only. All activity is
monitored and recorded. Unauthorized access is prohibited and will
be prosecuted to the fullest extent of the law. By continuing past
this banner you agree to these terms.

Bad example (avoid):

Welcome to Router1 at Building A!
Cisco ISR 4321 / IOS 15.7(3)M2
Call Bob at x1234 for help.

This banner violates every guideline — uses 'Welcome', reveals device details, exposes contact info.

hack.memory: MOTD rules: YES legal warning. NO 'Welcome'. NO device info. Lawyers hate 'Welcome' — it sounds like an invitation. Attackers love hostnames and versions — don't give them away.
hack.practice: Look up the DOJ's 'sample warning banner' template. Configure it verbatim on a lab router. Compare with a bad example and understand why each rule exists.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Content rules are testable — know the 'no Welcome, no device info' rule.

5.3.h.info:
The banner syntax uses a delimiter character to mark the beginning and end of the banner text. The delimiter can be any character that does NOT appear within the banner text itself.

Basic syntax:

banner motd [delimiter] [banner text] [delimiter]

Common delimiters: #, $, ^, *. The convention is # but any character works.

Single-line banner:

R1(config)# banner motd # Unauthorized access prohibited. #

Multi-line banner: after typing the opening delimiter, press Enter — IOS prompts with Enter TEXT message. End with the character '#'. You can type multiple lines until you type the closing delimiter alone on a line (or at the end of a line).

R1(config)# banner motd #
Enter TEXT message.  End with the character '#'.
WARNING: This system is for authorized use only.
All activity is monitored and recorded.
Unauthorized access will be prosecuted.
#
R1(config)#

Delimiter conflict gotcha: if your banner text contains the character #, you must choose a different delimiter:

banner motd $
Enter TEXT message. End with the character '$'.
Contact Helpdesk at ticket #12345 for access.
$

If you used # as delimiter with 'ticket #12345' in the banner, IOS would end the banner at the first # in '12345', resulting in 'Contact Helpdesk at ticket ' — truncated unexpectedly.

Viewing the configured banner: show running-config | begin banner or show banner motd.

Removing a banner: no banner motd deletes the banner. There is no way to 'edit' a banner — you must re-enter it completely.

hack.memory: banner motd [DELIM] text [DELIM]. # is the convention. Pick any char not in your text. Multi-line OK — end with the same delimiter on its own.
hack.practice: Configure a single-line and a multi-line banner. Intentionally include # in the text and watch it truncate with # as delimiter. Change the delimiter to $ and retry — works correctly.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Exam tests the delimiter concept — know any character can be used, and it can't appear in the text.

5.3.i.info:
The security passwords min-length [0-16] global configuration command enforces a minimum character length for all passwords configured subsequently on the device. It is a proactive control that prevents admins from setting dangerously short passwords.

Command syntax:

Router(config)# security passwords min-length 10

This sets the minimum password length to 10 characters. Any attempt to configure a shorter password (via enable secret, username X secret Y, or line password) will be rejected with an error.

Behavior:

  • Range: 0 to 16 characters (16 is the maximum IOS will enforce natively)
  • Cisco recommends at least 10 characters for production
  • Applies to ALL password types: enable password/secret, line passwords, username entries
  • Prospective only — does NOT retroactively invalidate existing passwords that are already shorter. Only enforces the minimum on new or changed passwords going forward.

Example:

Router(config)# security passwords min-length 10
Router(config)# enable secret abc
% Password too short - must be at least 10 characters.
% Password configuration failed
Router(config)# enable secret MyStrongPassword
Router(config)#   <-- success, 16 chars

What it does not enforce:

  • No complexity rules — cannot require uppercase, lowercase, numbers, symbols
  • No dictionary checks — 'passwordpassword' (16 chars) is accepted even though it is trivially guessable
  • No rotation or expiry
  • No reuse prevention

For enterprise-grade password policies: IOS min-length is a baseline. True policy enforcement (complexity, rotation, lockout, reuse) requires an AAA server (RADIUS/TACACS+) or a local policy engine like Cisco ISE.

hack.memory: security passwords min-length N = bouncer at the door checking ID. Rejects new passwords shorter than N. Doesn't kick out existing short passwords — only affects new ones. Range 0-16, recommended 10+.
hack.practice: Lab: security passwords min-length 10. Try enable secret abc — rejected. Try enable secret LongEnoughPass — accepted. Check if old shorter passwords still work (they do — not retroactive).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam tests exact syntax and the non-retroactive behavior.

5.3.i.info:
security passwords min-length [value] is the exact command — Cisco is specific about the wording. Variations that look similar but are wrong:

  • password min-length 10 ❌ — not a valid command
  • security password min-length 10 ❌ — 'password' should be plural ('passwords')
  • min-password-length 10 ❌ — invented syntax

Only security passwords min-length [0-16] works. Memorize the exact wording.

Effects across password types:

Router(config)# security passwords min-length 10
Router(config)# enable password short           <-- rejected
Router(config)# enable secret shorty            <-- rejected
Router(config)# username test secret tiny       <-- rejected
Router(config)# line con 0
Router(config-line)# password tinypw            <-- rejected
Router(config)# enable secret ThisIsLongEnough  <-- accepted (16 chars)

All four password-setting commands are subject to the min-length.

Disabling: no security passwords min-length — removes the enforcement. Existing passwords are unaffected; new passwords no longer have a minimum requirement.

Verification:

Router# show running-config | include security passwords
security passwords min-length 10

hack.memory: Exact command: security passwords min-length N. Plural 'passwords'. Range 0-16. Cisco recommends 10+. Memorize this exact syntax.
hack.practice: Type the command three different ways wrong, then the right way. Verify only the correct syntax works. Makes the exact wording stick.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Exam may ask the exact command — know it character-perfect.

5.3.i.info:
A critical behavioral quirk of security passwords min-length: it only enforces the minimum on new or changed passwords configured AFTER the command is applied. Existing passwords that were shorter than the new minimum remain valid and continue to work — they are not retroactively invalidated.

Example:

! Step 1 — configure a short password
Router(config)# enable secret abc       <-- 3 chars, accepted (no min-length yet)

! Step 2 — set min-length to 10
Router(config)# security passwords min-length 10

! Step 3 — the existing 3-char password still works
Router(config)# exit
Router# disable
Router> enable
Password: abc                            <-- accepted, old password still valid
Router#

! Step 4 — but new passwords must be >=10
Router(config)# enable secret xyz        <-- rejected, too short
Router(config)# enable secret LongerPass <-- accepted

Why this design: retroactively invalidating passwords would potentially lock all admins out of all devices at once — a disaster if the command is pushed broadly. The non-retroactive design lets admins enable min-length enforcement safely, then update existing passwords at their own pace.

Operational implication: after enabling min-length, you should audit existing passwords and manually update any that are below the new minimum. This is a two-step process:

  1. Enable security passwords min-length 10
  2. Update each existing weak password: enable secret [new strong password], username X secret [new strong password], etc.

Exam trap: 'After running security passwords min-length 10, is an existing 6-character password still usable?' Answer: YES — the command is not retroactive.

hack.memory: min-length = NOT retroactive. Old passwords keep working. Only new ones are checked. Rule of thumb: enable the command, then audit + update existing passwords manually.
hack.practice: Configure enable secret abc first. Then apply security passwords min-length 10. Log out, log back in with 'abc' — still works. Try to set a new short password — rejected. Confirms non-retroactive behavior.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Exam tests this specific behavior — 'does it retroactively enforce?' = NO.

5.3.j.info:
The login block-for [seconds] attempts [count] within [seconds] command provides brute-force login protection by temporarily blocking ALL login attempts after too many consecutive failures within a defined time window. It is a baseline security control for VTY lines exposed to untrusted networks.

Command anatomy:

login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60

Read as: 'If 3 failed login attempts occur within 60 seconds, block ALL logins for 120 seconds.' The three parameters:

  • block-for [seconds] — lockout duration
  • attempts [count] — failures that trigger the lockout
  • within [seconds] — the observation window for counting failures

Quiet mode: during the lockout period (Cisco calls this 'quiet mode'), IOS refuses ALL login attempts — including legitimate admin attempts. To prevent self-lockout, configure a whitelist:

login quiet-mode access-class TRUSTED_ADMINS

Trusted source IPs (per the ACL) can still log in during quiet mode.

Related commands:

  • login delay [seconds] — forces a minimum delay between login attempts (slows brute-force)
  • login on-failure log — generates a syslog message on failed logins (for monitoring/alerting)
  • login on-success log — generates a syslog message on successful logins (audit trail)

What it protects: primarily VTY (SSH/Telnet) brute-force password guessing. Without it, an attacker can try thousands of passwords per minute. With login block-for 600 attempts 3 within 60, the attacker gets 3 tries, then is locked out for 10 minutes — reducing the attack rate to ~432 attempts per day instead of tens of thousands per minute.

Status check: show login reveals current quiet-mode status, fail count, and time remaining in lockout.

hack.memory: Read it as a sentence: 'block FOR 120 seconds after 3 ATTEMPTS WITHIN 60 seconds.' Three parameters, always in that order: block-for → attempts → within. Quiet-mode ACL is the admin escape hatch.
hack.practice: Lab: login block-for 60 attempts 3 within 30. Intentionally fail 3 SSH logins quickly. Verify show login shows quiet mode active. Wait 60 seconds, confirm access returns.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam tests exact syntax and parameter order. Memorize it character-perfect.

5.3.j.info:
login block-for is a global configuration command (NOT line-specific — it applies to all VTY lines at once). The full syntax:

login block-for [1-65535] attempts [1-65535] within [1-3600]

Parameters:

  • block-for — seconds the device rejects logins (1 to 65535 seconds, max ~18 hours)
  • attempts — number of consecutive failed logins that trigger the lockout
  • within — time window (seconds) in which the failures must occur (1 to 3600 seconds, max 1 hour)

Practical examples:

  • login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60 — 3 fails in 60s → 120s lockout (gentle)
  • login block-for 600 attempts 3 within 60 — 3 fails in 60s → 10-min lockout (stronger)
  • login block-for 1800 attempts 5 within 120 — 5 fails in 2 min → 30-min lockout (aggressive)

Behavior during quiet mode:

  • ALL incoming login attempts rejected (including correct credentials from a legitimate admin)
  • Unless login quiet-mode access-class is configured with a whitelist
  • Syslog message generated at lockout start and end

Verification:

Router# show login
     A default login delay of 1 seconds is applied.
     No Quiet-Mode access list has been configured.

     Router enabled to watch for login Attacks.
     If more than 3 login failures occur in 60 seconds or less,
     logins will be disabled for 120 seconds.

     Router presently in Normal-Mode.
     Current Watch Window
         Time remaining: 46 seconds.
         Login failures for current window: 1.
     Total login failures: 2.

This shows thresholds, current mode, and failure counters.

hack.memory: Global command, not under line vty. Three numbers in fixed order: BLOCK-FOR, ATTEMPTS, WITHIN. 'show login' reveals current state. Optional ACL protects trusted admins from lockout.
hack.practice: Configure in lab. Run show login before any failures — shows normal mode + thresholds. Fail a few logins, watch counter increment. Hit threshold — mode switches to Quiet-Mode with countdown.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Exam requires exact parameter order — memorize the BLOCK-FOR → ATTEMPTS → WITHIN order.

5.3.j.info:
The canonical exam example: login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60. Walk through what happens second-by-second to cement the behavior.

Scenario timeline:

T+0   : Attacker starts SSH brute-force
T+0   : Attempt 1 — wrong password → FAIL (counter: 1)
T+15  : Attempt 2 — wrong password → FAIL (counter: 2)
T+30  : Attempt 3 — wrong password → FAIL (counter: 3)
