T+30  : Threshold hit → QUIET MODE activated
T+30  : Syslog: 'Quiet-Mode ON — all logins blocked'
T+60  : Attempt 4 (even with correct password) → REJECTED
T+90  : Attempt 5 — REJECTED
T+120 : Legitimate admin attempt — still REJECTED
T+150 : Quiet mode expires → normal mode resumes
T+150 : Counter resets to 0
T+151 : Legitimate admin attempt → SUCCESS

Key observations:

  • Counter resets to 0 when quiet mode ends
  • Counter also resets if the 'within' window expires without reaching the threshold (e.g., if the 3rd failure was at T+70 instead of T+30, the first failure at T+0 would have aged out of the 60-second window, so the count would still be 2)
  • During quiet mode, the CORRECT password is still rejected — the lockout is time-based, not credential-based

Whitelist exception: if login quiet-mode access-class TRUSTED is configured and the admin's source IP is permitted by ACL TRUSTED, the admin can still log in during quiet mode.

Exam trick: questions often give specific numbers and ask you to trace the sequence. Practice reading the command as: 'Block for X seconds after Y attempts within Z seconds.'

hack.memory: 120/3/60 = 'block 120, 3 fails, in 60 seconds.' During quiet mode, even correct password is rejected. Timer-based lockout. Counter resets when quiet mode ends or the 'within' window expires.
hack.practice: Walk through the timeline on paper with specific second marks. Then do it in lab — fail 3 times fast, try 4th (rejected even if correct), wait out the 120 seconds, confirm 5th attempt works.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Practice sequencing questions — exam presents timelines and asks what happens next.

5.3.j.info:
The parameter order in the login block-for command is fixed and non-negotiable: block-for, then attempts, then within. Cisco will not accept the keywords in any other order.

Correct syntax (only valid order):

login block-for [block-seconds] attempts [count] within [window-seconds]

Examples that WORK:

  • login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60
  • login block-for 600 attempts 5 within 120
  • login block-for 1800 attempts 10 within 300

Examples that FAIL (wrong order):

  • login attempts 3 block-for 120 within 60 ❌
  • login within 60 attempts 3 block-for 120 ❌
  • login block-for 120 within 60 attempts 3 ❌

Memory tricks for the order:

  • BAW — Block-for, Attempts, Within
  • 'Block the Bad guys, count the Attempts, inside the Window'
  • Alphabetical-ish: B → A → W (not strictly alphabetical but close)

Related commands (separate lines, not same command):

login block-for 120 attempts 3 within 60       <-- the main rate limit
login delay 3                                    <-- 3-second delay between attempts
login on-failure log                             <-- syslog on each failure
login on-success log                             <-- syslog on each success
login quiet-mode access-class TRUSTED_ADMINS     <-- whitelist during lockout

These are separate commands and can be in any order among themselves. Only within the login block-for command is the BAW order mandatory.

Exam pattern: the exam may show a multiple-choice question with the same command but keywords in different orders — only one is syntactically correct. Always pick BAW.

hack.memory: BAW order: Block-for, Attempts, Within. Fixed. Any other order = syntax error. Memorize the sequence like a ZIP code.
hack.practice: Type the command in the wrong order three times, confirm each fails. Then type the correct BAW order — accepted. Lock the sequence in muscle memory.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 52. Exam CLI questions punish wrong order — practice typing the exact sequence.

5.9.a.info:
WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy) was the original 802.11 wireless security standard, introduced in 1997. Its name reflects the design goal: to provide confidentiality on a wireless link 'equivalent' to the implicit protection of wired Ethernet. In practice, WEP was completely broken by 2001 and every version has been considered obsolete since 2004. It should never be used on any modern network.

Technical design:

  • Cipher: RC4 stream cipher
  • Initialization Vector (IV): only 24 bits — 2^24 ≈ 16.7 million possible values
  • Key sizes: 40-bit (original WEP) or 104-bit (WEP2 / 'WEP-128'). The '64-bit' and '128-bit' marketing numbers include the 24-bit IV (so actual shared key is 40 or 104 bits).
  • Static pre-shared keys: all clients share the same key; keys rarely rotate
  • Authentication: Open (no auth) or Shared Key (weak challenge-response)

Why WEP fails:

  • The 24-bit IV is so small that on a busy network, IVs repeat within hours — sometimes within minutes
  • When an IV repeats with the same static key, the RC4 keystream is reused, enabling statistical cryptanalysis (Fluhrer, Mantin, Shamir attack, 2001)
  • Tools like aircrack-ng can recover a WEP key in under 5 minutes given enough captured packets with repeated IVs
  • Modern attacks don't even need many packets — the 'PTW attack' needs as few as 40,000 frames, which a busy AP produces in seconds

Wireless security evolution timeline:

  1. WEP (1997) — broken
  2. WPA / TKIP (2003) — transitional, now deprecated
  3. WPA2 / AES-CCMP (2004, mandatory 2006) — current minimum
  4. WPA3 / SAE, OWE (2018) — newest, strongest

hack.memory: WEP = Weak Encryption Protocol (unofficial but true). 24-bit IV → collisions → crackable in minutes. NEVER use. Only reason it is on the exam is so you can identify it and reject it.
hack.practice: Memorize the timeline: WEP (RC4/24-bit IV, broken) → WPA (TKIP/RC4, deprecated) → WPA2 (AES/CCMP, current) → WPA3 (SAE/OWE, newest). Know the cipher per generation.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam tests WHY WEP is broken — answer is 24-bit IV reuse enabling key recovery.

5.9.a.info:
The essential facts about WEP that must be recallable on the exam:

  • Year introduced: 1997 (part of original 802.11)
  • Cipher: RC4 stream cipher
  • IV size: 24 bits
  • Shared key sizes: 40-bit (called '64-bit' WEP) or 104-bit (called '128-bit' WEP)
  • Status: deprecated by Wi-Fi Alliance in 2004; completely broken
  • Replacement: WPA (2003), then WPA2 (2004)
  • Attack tools: aircrack-ng, airodump-ng, aireplay-ng — standard in every security-tester's toolkit

Why it's still on the exam: CCNA candidates need to recognize WEP in configurations and know to reject it. You may see a question like 'A client wants to secure a wireless network for retail point-of-sale devices. Which of these options is the worst choice?' — WEP is the wrong answer (i.e., the one to avoid).

Spotting WEP in configs: WLC GUI shows WEP options under Layer 2 Security as 'Static WEP' or 'Dynamic WEP (802.1X)'. Either should be disabled on modern deployments. Legacy IoT devices (old printers, barcode scanners, warehouse PDAs) sometimes still require WEP — they should be isolated on a dedicated, heavily segmented VLAN.

Compliance angle: WEP fails PCI DSS, HIPAA, NIST 800-53, and essentially every modern security standard. Using WEP on a payment network is an automatic compliance failure.

hack.memory: WEP = RC4 + 24-bit IV + static keys + 1997. Everything about it is old and broken. On the exam, WEP is always the wrong answer.
hack.practice: Read a WLC screenshot that offers WEP, WPA, WPA2, WPA3. Know WEP is the weakest. Memorize the four security options and their relative strength.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Exam tests recognition — if you see WEP in an answer choice for 'secure network,' it's wrong.

5.9.a.info:
The 24-bit Initialization Vector (IV) is WEP's fatal flaw. An IV is a random value added to each encrypted frame to ensure that encrypting the same plaintext twice produces different ciphertext. In WEP, the IV is sent in cleartext alongside the encrypted frame — the receiver uses it (combined with the shared key) to decrypt.

Why 24 bits is too small:

  • 2^24 = 16,777,216 — about 16.7 million unique IVs
  • A busy AP transmitting 1,000 frames per second exhausts the IV space in ~4.6 hours
  • Even on a quieter link (100 fps), IVs repeat within 46 hours
  • Many APs reset IV counters on reboot, leading to even earlier collisions
  • WEP does not coordinate IV selection between devices — random IVs collide by the birthday paradox after roughly 2^12 = 4,096 frames (seconds of traffic)

What an attacker does when IVs collide:

  1. Capture a large volume of frames (aircrack-ng with aireplay-ng to inject, speeding this up)
  2. Detect frames with repeated IVs
  3. Use statistical analysis (FMS attack, KoreK attack, PTW attack) to derive the shared key from IV collisions
  4. With the key, decrypt all traffic — past and future

Comparison with later standards:

  • WEP IV: 24 bits
  • TKIP IV (WPA): 48 bits — 2^24 times more IV space, eliminating reuse in practice
  • AES-CCMP IV (WPA2): 48-bit Packet Number, never reused for a given key

The IV size increase from 24 to 48 bits alone is enough to defeat the IV-collision class of attacks. But WPA/TKIP also added per-packet key mixing and MIC to fix other WEP flaws.

hack.memory: 24 bits = too small → IVs repeat → RC4 keystream reuses → statistical analysis → key recovered. The IV size alone killed WEP. WPA+ uses 48-bit IV = 16.7 million times more space.
hack.practice: Calculate: at 1000 frames/sec, how long until a 24-bit IV space is exhausted? (16.7M / 1000 = 16,777 seconds ≈ 4.6 hours). Now compare 48-bit: 281 trillion / 1000 = ~8900 years. That size jump is why WPA works.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam tests the IV concept — why 24 bits is too small.

5.9.a.info:
WEP uses a static pre-shared key — every client on the network has the same key, and the key rarely (if ever) changes. This design makes WEP hard to scale and creates a permanent security liability.

Problems with static keys:

  • No per-user keys — if one device is compromised (stolen laptop, malicious insider, infected IoT device), the attacker has the key for the entire network
  • No key rotation — the same key is used for months or years; long-term captures accumulate enough data to crack it
  • Manual rekey — to rotate the key, every device on the network must be reconfigured manually
  • Key distribution is informal — keys get shared via email, sticky notes, or verbally, leading to leaks

Contrast with modern standards:

  • WPA2-PSK: still uses a pre-shared passphrase, but each session derives unique session keys via the 4-way handshake (so different clients use different real encryption keys)
  • WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X): each user authenticates with individual credentials; the RADIUS server generates unique session keys per user per session
  • WPA3-SAE: replaces the PSK handshake with Dragonfly key exchange, provides forward secrecy (past session keys stay safe even if current PSK leaks)

Real-world scenario: a retail store uses WEP across 200 barcode scanners. An employee's scanner is stolen. The thief extracts the WEP key from the scanner's config. Now the thief can decrypt all wireless traffic — past captures and future traffic — until the WEP key is rotated across ALL 200 scanners manually.

hack.memory: WEP static key = one key everyone shares. Lose it once, compromise everything. No per-session keys, no rotation. That alone makes it unsuitable for any network with more than a handful of trusted devices.
hack.practice: Imagine rotating a WEP key across 500 devices manually. Understand why static PSKs at scale are a disaster — and why 802.1X / per-session keys replaced them.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Static-key weakness is one of three core reasons WEP was deprecated.

5.9.b.info:
WPA (Wi-Fi Protected Access) was introduced in 2003 as an emergency fix for WEP. The goal: ship a significantly more secure wireless protocol that would run on existing WEP-era hardware with only a firmware update — no forklift upgrade required. WPA achieved this using TKIP (Temporal Key Integrity Protocol) as a wrapper around the same RC4 stream cipher that WEP used.

What TKIP added over WEP:

  • 48-bit IV (vs WEP's 24-bit) — 2^48 = 281 trillion unique values, eliminating IV reuse in practice
  • Per-packet key mixing — each frame is encrypted with a unique key derived from the master key + sender MAC + sequence number. Recovering one packet's key tells an attacker nothing about any other packet.
  • Message Integrity Check (MIC) — called 'Michael' — detects packet tampering. If MIC failures are detected twice within a minute, the AP forces a rekey and temporarily disables the network. Prevents simple forgery.
  • Sequence counter — prevents replay attacks. Packets received out of order are rejected.

Why TKIP was only transitional:

  • Still uses RC4 — an aging stream cipher with known weaknesses
  • Beck-Tews attack (2008) can inject arbitrary packets into TKIP-protected networks in ~15 minutes
  • TKIP is now officially deprecated — 802.11-2012 marked it deprecated, and 802.11-2016 disallows TKIP in WPA2 networks
  • Modern Wi-Fi Alliance certifications require AES-CCMP, not TKIP

WPA naming: 'WPA' without a number refers to the original 2003 version with TKIP. 'WPA2' (2004) introduced AES-CCMP. The naming is confusing because 'WPA' is both a marketing umbrella and a specific version name.

Modern guidance: never configure WPA/TKIP on new deployments. If an AP offers 'WPA+WPA2' mixed mode, disable WPA/TKIP and require WPA2/AES only.

hack.memory: WPA = Band-aid for WEP. TKIP = Temporary Key Integrity Protocol (name literally says TEMPORARY). Still RC4 but with 48-bit IV + per-packet keys + MIC. Better than WEP but now deprecated.
hack.practice: Build a comparison table: WEP vs WPA vs WPA2. WEP = RC4+24-bit IV. WPA = TKIP wraps RC4 + 48-bit IV + MIC. WPA2 = AES-CCMP (totally new cipher). Memorize which cipher belongs to which generation.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam tests the WEP→WPA→WPA2→WPA3 evolution and which cipher each uses.

5.9.b.info:
WPA (version 1) is the 2003 Wi-Fi Alliance security standard that implemented the IEEE 802.11i draft on existing WEP hardware. It was always a bridge — never meant to be a long-term solution. By 2006 it was superseded by WPA2 (based on the finalized 802.11i), and by 2018 by WPA3.

WPA specifics:

  • Cipher: RC4 (same as WEP, wrapped by TKIP)
  • Key management: TKIP adds per-packet key derivation and automatic key rotation
  • Two modes: WPA-Personal (PSK) and WPA-Enterprise (802.1X)
  • Handshake: 4-way handshake derives session keys (PTK) from the master key (PMK)
  • IV size: 48 bits (vs WEP's 24)
  • Integrity: Michael MIC (vs WEP's weak CRC-32)

Current status:

  • Deprecated by IEEE 802.11-2012
  • Prohibited in Wi-Fi Alliance WPA2 certification requirements
  • Still appears on some WLC GUI dropdowns for backward compatibility — should be unchecked

Known attacks on TKIP:

  • Beck-Tews attack (2008) — packet injection and decryption of short ARP packets in ~15 minutes
  • Ohigashi-Morii attack (2009) — improved Beck-Tews, works in ~1 minute
  • These attacks do NOT recover the master PSK but can inject malicious frames — still serious

WLC configuration note: on a Cisco WLC, when configuring a WLAN's Layer 2 Security as 'WPA+WPA2', you can individually enable/disable WPA Policy and WPA2 Policy. Best practice: uncheck WPA Policy, check WPA2 Policy, set encryption to AES only.

hack.memory: WPA v1 = TKIP + RC4. Temporary. DEPRECATED. If you see it on a WLC GUI, disable it. WPA2 + AES is the minimum.
hack.practice: On a WLC GUI, navigate to WLAN Security Layer 2. Find the WPA Policy checkbox. Uncheck it. Leave WPA2 Policy checked. That's the production config.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Exam may ask 'which cipher does WPA use?' = TKIP (wrapping RC4).

5.9.b.info:
TKIP (Temporal Key Integrity Protocol) was designed with one specific constraint: must run on existing WEP hardware with only a firmware update. This meant TKIP had to continue using the RC4 stream cipher — because WEP chipsets had RC4 in hardware, and replacing those chipsets would require replacing every AP and client in the world.

What TKIP accomplished under this constraint:

  1. Eliminated IV reuse — expanded IV from 24 to 48 bits. Eliminates the WEP IV-collision attack class entirely.
  2. Added per-packet keys — each frame encrypted with a unique RC4 key derived from: master key + sender MAC + sequence counter. Even if the attacker breaks one packet, the next packet uses a different key.
  3. Added MIC (Michael) — a cryptographic integrity check that detects tampering. Replaces WEP's weak CRC-32 which didn't actually provide integrity protection.
  4. Automatic rekeying — the master key rotates periodically (every few hours by default), limiting long-term exposure.

TKIP's fundamental limitation: no matter how cleverly you wrap RC4, you are still using a 20-year-old stream cipher with inherent weaknesses. The Beck-Tews attack (2008) exploited weaknesses in Michael MIC and TKIP's countermeasures to inject packets.

Why WPA2 dropped TKIP: once hardware with AES acceleration became affordable (~2004-2006), there was no reason to continue with RC4. AES-CCMP provides both stronger encryption (AES block cipher) and stronger integrity (CBC-MAC in CCMP) in a single protocol, without TKIP's historical baggage.

Exam framing: 'What problem did TKIP solve?' — answer: made it possible to deploy 802.11i-class security on existing WEP hardware via a firmware update. 'What cipher does TKIP use?' — RC4. 'Why is TKIP no longer recommended?' — because AES-CCMP is available and TKIP has known attacks.

hack.memory: TKIP = 'Temporary' fix. Kept RC4 for hardware compat but added 48-bit IV, per-packet keys, MIC, rekeying. Made old hardware secure enough to ship. Replaced by AES-CCMP once new hardware shipped.
hack.practice: Study the four TKIP improvements: larger IV, per-packet keys, MIC, rekey. Each addresses a specific WEP flaw. Know what each one fixed.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam may test the 'hardware compatibility' rationale for TKIP.

5.9.b.info:
The wireless encryption cipher lineage is a high-frequency exam topic. Memorize each generation's primary cipher:

StandardYearCipherStatus
WEP1997RC4 + 24-bit IVBROKEN
WPA2003TKIP (wraps RC4)DEPRECATED
WPA22004 (mandatory 2006)AES-CCMPCURRENT MINIMUM
WPA32018AES-CCMP or AES-GCMP (with SAE, OWE)NEWEST

Key transitions to know:

  • WEP → WPA: kept RC4 but wrapped it in TKIP with larger IV, per-packet keys, and MIC
  • WPA → WPA2: replaced RC4/TKIP entirely with AES/CCMP — completely different cipher, not a wrapper
  • WPA2 → WPA3: kept AES but replaced the 4-way handshake with SAE (Dragonfly) for PSK mode, added OWE for open networks, added 192-bit CNSA mode for enterprise

Mnemonic for the cipher ladder:

  • RC4 (weak stream cipher) → AES (strong block cipher). Crossing that line (WPA → WPA2) is the biggest single upgrade.
  • TKIP and CCMP are wrappers/protocols around the underlying cipher
  • CCMP = Counter Mode with CBC-MAC Protocol — does BOTH encryption (Counter Mode) AND integrity (CBC-MAC) in one protocol

What changes at each transition:

  • WEP → WPA: IV 24→48, keys static→per-packet, integrity CRC-32→Michael MIC
  • WPA → WPA2: RC4→AES (block cipher replaces stream cipher), TKIP→CCMP
  • WPA2 → WPA3: 4-way handshake→SAE, open network plaintext→OWE encryption, 128-bit→optional 192-bit

hack.memory: RC4 → RC4-wrapped → AES → AES-with-better-handshake. The big jump is WPA→WPA2 (RC4 dies, AES takes over). The next big jump is WPA2→WPA3 (handshake upgrade, open networks encrypted via OWE).
hack.practice: Memorize the table row by row. For each generation, recite cipher, year, status. Exam WILL ask which cipher belongs to which standard.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. High-frequency exam content.

5.9.c.info:
WPA2 is the current wireless security baseline — mandatory for Wi-Fi Alliance certification since 2006, based on the finalized IEEE 802.11i standard. WPA2's defining upgrade over WPA: it replaced the RC4/TKIP cipher suite with the far stronger AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) paired with CCMP (Counter Mode with CBC-MAC Protocol).

Technical details:

  • AES — symmetric block cipher, 128-bit keys, 128-bit blocks. AES is the gold-standard cipher used globally for classified government communications. No practical attack against AES itself has ever been demonstrated.
  • CCMP — combines two modes of AES: Counter Mode for encryption (turns AES block cipher into a stream cipher for efficiency) and CBC-MAC for integrity/authentication. A single protocol provides both confidentiality and integrity, replacing both RC4 encryption and Michael MIC.
  • 4-way handshake — client and AP exchange four EAPOL frames to derive session-specific encryption keys (PTK) from the shared PMK. PMK comes from the PSK (Personal mode) or the RADIUS server (Enterprise mode).

Two operating modes:

  • WPA2-Personal (PSK) — pre-shared passphrase. All users enter the same password. Simple, suitable for home/SOHO.
  • WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X) — per-user credentials via RADIUS. Required for corporate environments.

KRACK vulnerability (2017): Key Reinstallation Attack — a flaw in how devices handle the 4-way handshake allows an attacker within range to manipulate the handshake into reinstalling encryption keys, potentially enabling decryption of some traffic. KRACK was patched in software updates across all major OS and device vendors. WPA2 with current patches remains secure.

Compliance: WPA2-AES meets PCI DSS, HIPAA, NIST 800-53, and virtually every modern security standard. It is the minimum acceptable wireless security for any production network.

hack.memory: WPA2 = AES + CCMP. AES = the cipher that hasn't been broken. CCMP = one protocol that does BOTH encryption and integrity. Two modes: Personal (PSK) or Enterprise (802.1X).
hack.practice: Configure WPA2-PSK on a WLC in a lab/sim. Layer 2 Security = WPA+WPA2 → WPA2 Policy enabled → AES encryption → PSK auth → enter passphrase. Connect a client. Then try Enterprise mode with RADIUS.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Most heavily tested wireless security content on CCNA.

5.9.c.info:
WPA2 is the Wi-Fi Alliance's certification program based on the IEEE 802.11i-2004 amendment. Key facts:

  • Year introduced: 2004 (Wi-Fi Alliance WPA2 certification), with 802.11i-2004 standard finalized
  • Mandatory in all Wi-Fi certified products since 2006
  • Primary cipher: AES-CCMP (also supports TKIP for legacy compatibility, though TKIP is now deprecated)
  • Replaces: WEP (broken) and WPA (transitional)
  • Succeeded by: WPA3 (2018) — but WPA2 remains the baseline and is still acceptable for current deployments with proper configuration

Terminology:

  • IEEE 802.11i — the IEEE standard defining the security architecture
  • WPA2 — the Wi-Fi Alliance certification based on 802.11i
  • RSN (Robust Security Network) — the formal name in the 802.11 standard for the security framework that WPA2 implements
  • RSNA (Robust Security Network Association) — a specific client-AP security association using RSN

On Cisco WLC GUI, WPA2 appears as 'WPA2 Policy' under Layer 2 Security → WPA+WPA2. Checking this box enables WPA2 support on the WLAN.

Authentication Key Management (AKM) methods under WPA2:

  • PSK (pre-shared key) — Personal mode
  • 802.1X (EAP) — Enterprise mode
  • FT-PSK and FT-802.1X — Fast Transition (802.11r) variants for fast roaming

hack.memory: WPA2 = 802.11i = 2004/2006 = AES-CCMP = current minimum. Personal (PSK) for home. Enterprise (802.1X) for work. Still acceptable in 2026 with patches.
hack.practice: Identify WPA2 in a WLC screenshot. Know the two modes. Know the cipher (AES-CCMP). Drill the year and standard number.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Exam tests WPA2 = AES-CCMP = current standard.

5.9.c.info:
AES-CCMP is the cryptographic protocol at the heart of WPA2. It combines AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) — a NIST-approved symmetric block cipher — with CCMP (Counter Mode with CBC-MAC Protocol), which uses AES in two different modes simultaneously to provide both encryption and message integrity.

AES specifics:

  • Symmetric block cipher (same key for encrypt and decrypt)
  • 128-bit block size
  • Key sizes: 128, 192, or 256 bits. WPA2 uses 128-bit AES keys by default.
  • Rijndael algorithm, selected by NIST in 2001 after a 5-year competition
  • No practical cryptanalytic attack has ever been demonstrated against full-round AES

CCMP = CTR + CBC-MAC:

  • Counter Mode (CTR) — turns the AES block cipher into a stream cipher. Each block is encrypted by XORing plaintext against AES(key, counter) where counter increments per block. Efficient and parallelizable.
  • CBC-MAC — computes a cryptographic checksum over the plaintext to provide integrity/authentication. If any bit is flipped in transit, the receiver detects it.
  • Combined: every WPA2 frame is both encrypted AND authenticated using AES, with a single key.

Why CCMP replaced TKIP:

  • AES is a block cipher, not a stream cipher — not vulnerable to the RC4 weaknesses that plagued WEP/TKIP
  • CCMP provides integrity via CBC-MAC (cryptographically strong) vs Michael MIC (breakable)
  • Single protocol for both confidentiality and integrity (simpler, more efficient)
  • Hardware acceleration — modern AP/client chipsets include dedicated AES acceleration, making CCMP faster than software TKIP on the same hardware

Frame format: a CCMP-protected frame includes an 8-byte CCMP header (with 48-bit Packet Number that prevents replay), encrypted payload, and 8-byte MIC.

hack.memory: AES = the strong cipher. CCMP = the protocol that uses AES for BOTH encryption (Counter Mode) AND integrity (CBC-MAC). One protocol, one cipher, one key, two jobs. Replaces TKIP + Michael.
hack.practice: Memorize the expansion: CCMP = Counter Mode with CBC-MAC Protocol. AES = Advanced Encryption Standard. Know which part does encryption (Counter Mode) and which does integrity (CBC-MAC).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. AES-CCMP is the single most tested WPA2 term.

5.9.c.info:
WPA2-AES is the minimum acceptable wireless security for any production network in 2026. Deploying anything weaker (WEP, WPA/TKIP, or open networks without OWE) fails every modern security standard and creates unacceptable risk.

Compliance mandates:

  • PCI DSS (payment card industry): requires 'strong encryption' — WEP and WPA/TKIP explicitly forbidden since PCI DSS 3.0 (2015). WPA2-AES is the minimum.
  • HIPAA (healthcare): no specific cipher named, but 'addressable' encryption safeguards require industry-standard strength — WPA2-AES is the practical minimum.
  • NIST 800-53: cryptographic controls require FIPS-validated AES. TKIP is not FIPS-approved.
  • CJIS (Criminal Justice Information Services): requires 128-bit AES minimum. Only WPA2-AES or WPA3 qualify.

Deployment guidance:

  • New networks (2026): deploy WPA3 where all clients support it. Fall back to WPA3 Transition Mode (WPA2+WPA3 coexisting) for mixed fleets.
  • Existing WPA2 networks: acceptable to maintain, but plan migration to WPA3 as client support expands.
  • Never acceptable: open networks without OWE, WEP (any variant), WPA/TKIP.

WLC configuration guidance: when configuring WPA2 on a Cisco WLC:

  • Layer 2 Security: WPA+WPA2
  • WPA Policy: UNCHECKED (disables WPA v1 with TKIP)
  • WPA2 Policy: CHECKED
  • WPA2 Encryption: AES ONLY (uncheck TKIP)
  • Authentication Key Management: PSK (Personal) or 802.1X (Enterprise)

This configuration ensures only WPA2-AES is offered; legacy TKIP is blocked.

hack.memory: Rule: WPA2-AES or better, always. WEP and TKIP are NEVER acceptable. WPA3 is the goal for new deployments, but WPA2-AES is still the minimum baseline.
hack.practice: Audit a WLC config. Check each WLAN — verify WPA2 Policy is on, WPA Policy is off, AES is the only cipher, TKIP is disabled. That's the target state.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam: 'What is the minimum acceptable wireless security?' = WPA2-AES.

5.9.d.info:
WPA3 is the newest Wi-Fi Alliance security standard (introduced 2018), designed to address the remaining weaknesses of WPA2. It introduces three major security improvements, each solving a specific problem.

1) SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) — Dragonfly key exchange

  • Replaces WPA2's PSK 4-way handshake
  • Resistant to offline dictionary attacks: even if an attacker captures the handshake, they cannot take it offline and brute-force the PSK. Each guess requires a real-time exchange with the AP, making attacks exponentially slower.
  • Provides forward secrecy — compromising the PSK today does not expose past session traffic. Each session generates unique ephemeral keys.
  • Protects against passive sniffing — a captured WPA3 handshake is useless for later cracking.

2) OWE (Opportunistic Wireless Encryption)

  • Provides encryption on open networks (coffee shops, airports, hotels) without requiring a password
  • Under WPA2, open networks broadcast everything in cleartext — anyone with a wireless sniffer (Wireshark + monitor mode) can read your traffic
  • OWE uses Diffie-Hellman to establish a session key between each client and the AP, encrypting the traffic even though there is no authentication
  • Protects against passive eavesdropping; does NOT protect against active man-in-the-middle (that requires authentication)

3) Protected Management Frames (PMF) — 802.11w

  • Mandatory in WPA3 (optional in WPA2)
  • Encrypts management frames (beacons, deauthentications, disassociations)
  • Prevents deauth attacks — attacks where a malicious device sends forged deauth frames to disconnect legitimate clients

4) WPA3-Enterprise 192-bit mode

  • Optional enhanced security level for government, military, and financial use
  • Uses CNSA (Commercial National Security Algorithm) suite: AES-GCMP-256, HMAC-SHA384, ECDSA/ECDHE with 384-bit curves
  • Meets CNSA / NSA Suite B requirements for classified information protection

hack.memory: WPA3 adds four things: SAE (kills offline PSK attacks), OWE (encrypts open WiFi), PMF (encrypts mgmt frames), 192-bit (government-grade option). Memorize these four features.
hack.practice: List what WPA3 fixes vs WPA2. For each fix, name the specific threat eliminated. Offline PSK cracking → SAE. Cleartext open networks → OWE. Deauth attacks → PMF.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. WPA3 is on the current CCNA blueprint — expect questions about SAE, OWE, and the 192-bit option.

5.9.d.info:
WPA3 was announced by the Wi-Fi Alliance in January 2018 and became mandatory for Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax) certification in July 2020. It preserves the AES-CCMP cipher (and adds optional AES-GCMP-256 for enterprise) but upgrades the key exchange, mandates PMF, and adds encryption for open networks.

Two modes (mirrors WPA2):

  • WPA3-Personal — uses SAE in place of PSK. Same user experience (enter a passphrase) but much stronger key exchange.
  • WPA3-Enterprise — uses 802.1X with either standard strength (AES-CCMP-128) or optional 192-bit CNSA mode (AES-GCMP-256).

Enhancements over WPA2:

  1. SAE key exchange (Personal mode) — immune to offline dictionary attack
  2. OWE for open networks — encrypts public Wi-Fi
  3. PMF mandatory — protects management frames
  4. Forward secrecy — past session keys stay safe even if current PSK leaks
  5. WPS removed — no more PIN brute-force
  6. 192-bit CNSA option (Enterprise)

Transition Mode (mixed WPA2/WPA3): because not all clients support WPA3 yet, most networks deploy in 'WPA2+WPA3 Transition Mode' where the AP advertises both — modern clients negotiate WPA3, legacy clients fall back to WPA2. This is a gradual migration path.

Known WPA3 issue (Dragonblood, 2019): early WPA3 implementations had timing and side-channel vulnerabilities in the SAE implementation. Patched in firmware updates. Current WPA3 is considered secure.

Deployment status (2026): Wi-Fi 6/6E/7 networks widely support WPA3. Client support (phones, laptops) is near-universal for devices less than 5 years old. Legacy IoT devices may still require WPA2.

hack.memory: WPA3 = 2018. Mandatory for Wi-Fi 6 cert. Personal uses SAE, Enterprise uses 802.1X with optional 192-bit. Transition Mode = WPA2+WPA3 coexist for gradual migration.
hack.practice: Identify WPA3 options on a modern WLC GUI. Understand Transition Mode for mixed-client fleets. Know the four WPA3 improvements cold.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Exam expects recall of WPA3's four major features.

5.9.d.info:
SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals), also known as Dragonfly, is WPA3-Personal's replacement for the WPA2 PSK 4-way handshake. It solves the offline dictionary attack that plagued WPA2-PSK.

The WPA2-PSK problem SAE solves:

  • Under WPA2-PSK, an attacker near the AP can capture the 4-way handshake when any client connects
  • With the captured handshake, the attacker can take it offline and brute-force the PSK — trying billions of passwords per second on a GPU
  • Weak passwords (dictionary words, short phrases) get cracked in minutes
  • Tools: hashcat, aircrack-ng, wordlists like rockyou.txt

How SAE prevents offline attacks:

  • SAE is a PAKE (Password-Authenticated Key Exchange) — specifically a variant of the Dragonfly PAKE
  • Instead of deriving a key from the password via a hash (which attackers can compute offline), SAE requires real-time interaction with the AP for every password guess
  • Each guess requires a round trip of handshake frames — attacks are rate-limited by the AP, not by GPU speed
  • An attacker who needs to try 1 billion passwords against a WPA3 AP would need 1 billion real-time handshake attempts — impossibly slow

Other SAE benefits:

  • Forward secrecy — each session uses ephemeral Diffie-Hellman keys. Compromising the PSK does NOT reveal past session traffic.
  • Equal peer authentication — both client and AP authenticate each other (no asymmetry like in EAP where only the server authenticates)

User experience: identical to WPA2-PSK. The user enters a passphrase on the client device; the complex crypto happens behind the scenes. Nothing extra to configure from the user's side.

hack.memory: SAE / Dragonfly = no more offline cracking of Wi-Fi passwords. Every guess requires talking to the AP in real time. Forward secrecy = past traffic safe even if PSK leaks. Replaces WPA2's broken 4-way handshake for Personal mode.
hack.practice: Compare WPA2-PSK attack (capture handshake offline, brute-force) vs WPA3-SAE attack (requires real-time round trip per guess). Understand why the second is dramatically slower.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam may ask 'what does SAE replace?' = PSK 4-way handshake.

5.9.d.info:
OWE (Opportunistic Wireless Encryption) provides encryption on open wireless networks — networks that have no password, like those in coffee shops, airports, hotels, and public Wi-Fi hotspots. Under WPA2 and earlier, open networks transmit all traffic in cleartext, which is why security experts have historically warned against using public Wi-Fi.

The problem OWE solves:

  • Open network at Starbucks — user browses, enters a password on a legacy HTTP site, checks email
  • Any attacker in the area runs Wireshark in monitor mode and sees everything
  • HTTPS protects HTTP content, but SSIDs, hostnames, and certain metadata still leak
  • DNS queries leak (until DoH/DoT) — attacker sees every website visited

How OWE works:

  • When a client connects to an open OWE network, the client and AP perform Diffie-Hellman key exchange to establish a shared encryption key
  • No password, no certificate — just ephemeral DH between client and AP
  • Each client session uses a unique key — one attacker cannot decrypt another client's traffic
  • Uses the same AES-CCMP or GCMP encryption as authenticated WPA3

What OWE protects against:

  • Passive eavesdropping — the #1 threat on public Wi-Fi. An attacker with a Wi-Fi adapter in monitor mode can no longer read your traffic.

What OWE does NOT protect against:

  • Active man-in-the-middle (MitM) — an attacker with a rogue AP can still intercept traffic because there's no authentication of the AP. End-to-end TLS (HTTPS) is still required for real protection.
  • Evil Twin — attacker spins up a fake AP with the same SSID; clients may connect unwittingly. Same mitigation as WPA2 (use a VPN).

OWE Transition Mode: OWE APs advertise a hidden OWE-only network alongside the visible open network. Legacy clients connect unencrypted to the open network; OWE-capable clients connect to the hidden encrypted network. Gradual migration path.

hack.memory: OWE = Opportunistic = encryption without a password. Uses Diffie-Hellman between client and AP. Protects against passive sniffing (Wireshark). Does NOT protect against active MitM — still need HTTPS/VPN.
hack.practice: Look up OWE implementations (Passpoint, Enhanced Open). Understand why 'encryption without authentication' is still useful — it stops the most common passive attack on public Wi-Fi.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Exam expects recall: OWE encrypts open networks without password, part of WPA3.

5.9.d.info:
WPA3-Enterprise 192-bit mode (also called 'WPA3-Enterprise Suite B' or 'CNSA mode') is an optional high-security configuration for WPA3-Enterprise, designed for environments that require the strongest cryptographic protection — government, military, financial, healthcare, and other regulated industries.

What 192-bit mode specifies:

  • AES-GCMP-256 — AES with 256-bit keys in Galois/Counter Mode with Message Authentication (replaces AES-CCMP-128 used in standard WPA3)
  • HMAC-SHA384 — stronger message authentication (replaces SHA-256)
  • ECDHE/ECDSA with P-384 curves — 384-bit elliptic curve cryptography (replaces P-256)
  • 192-bit effective security level — resistant to even quantum-computing-era attacks on classical crypto

CNSA (Commercial National Security Algorithm) suite: defined by NSA for protecting classified US government information. WPA3's 192-bit mode aligns with CNSA requirements, enabling Wi-Fi deployment for classified environments without needing exotic proprietary hardware.

Deployment:

  • Requires hardware support — not all AP/client chipsets implement AES-GCMP-256
  • Configured via WLC and RADIUS — clients must also support 192-bit mode or connections fail
  • Typical in military networks, financial trading floors, government facilities

Comparison with standard WPA3-Enterprise:

  • Standard: AES-CCMP-128, sufficient for corporate use
  • 192-bit: AES-GCMP-256, meets government/classified requirements

Exam framing: the CCNA generally treats 192-bit as 'the high-security option for government/financial.' You don't need to memorize the cipher details, but know:

  • It exists
  • It's part of WPA3-Enterprise, not WPA3-Personal
  • It uses 192-bit CNSA suite / AES-GCMP-256
  • Use case: classified/government/financial

hack.memory: WPA3-Enterprise 192-bit = the government-grade option. AES-GCMP-256, P-384 curves, CNSA suite. For classified environments only. Standard WPA3-Enterprise is fine for corporate.
hack.practice: Know the difference: standard WPA3-Enterprise (AES-CCMP-128) vs 192-bit mode (AES-GCMP-256 + CNSA). Use case: government and finance, not regular corporate.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Lower exam weight but mentioned. Know it exists and what it's for.

5.9.e.info:
Personal mode — also called WPA2-Personal or WPA3-Personal — uses a Pre-Shared Key (PSK): a single passphrase that every user must know to connect to the WLAN. It is the simpler of the two deployment models, suitable for home and small office networks where a centralized authentication server is impractical.

How WPA2-Personal works:

  1. Administrator configures a PSK (8-63 ASCII characters or 64 hexadecimal characters) on the AP/WLC
  2. Users enter the same PSK on their client devices
  3. Client hashes the PSK + SSID via PBKDF2 to derive the Pairwise Master Key (PMK)
  4. Client and AP perform the 4-way handshake using the PMK to derive unique session keys (PTK)
  5. Each client session uses different session keys even though all clients started from the same PSK

WPA3-Personal difference: uses SAE instead of the PSK 4-way handshake. User experience is identical (enter a passphrase), but the key exchange is cryptographically stronger and resistant to offline dictionary attacks.

PSK requirements:

  • Minimum 8 ASCII characters (Wi-Fi Alliance spec), or 64 hex characters
  • Maximum 63 ASCII characters
  • Recommended: 20+ characters with mixed character classes to resist dictionary attacks (WPA2) — less critical for WPA3 due to SAE

Pros and cons:

  • PRO — simple deployment: no server infrastructure required; just set a password on the AP
  • PRO — broad client support: every Wi-Fi capable device supports PSK mode
  • CON — no individual accountability: all users share the same password, so you can't track who did what
  • CON — single point of compromise: one leaked PSK compromises the entire network
  • CON — rekey pain: rotating the PSK requires manually updating every device
  • CON (WPA2 only) — offline dictionary attacks: weak passphrases can be brute-forced from a captured 4-way handshake. WPA3-SAE fixes this.

When to use Personal: home networks, small offices (< 10 users), IoT segments, guest networks (with appropriate isolation).

hack.memory: Personal = PSK = ONE SHARED PASSWORD for everyone. Like a house key copied 50 times — security depends on the least careful person. Home and small office only. No accountability. Rekey pain at scale.
hack.practice: Configure WPA2-Personal on a lab AP. Connect with the PSK. Try to change the PSK — confirm all existing devices disconnect and need reconfiguration. That's the scaling problem in action.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam: 'home network' → Personal. 'corporate with users' → Enterprise.

5.9.e.info:
Quick reference for Personal mode across WPA generations:

GenerationAuth methodKey exchangeCipher
WPA-PersonalPSK4-way handshakeTKIP
WPA2-PersonalPSK4-way handshakeAES-CCMP
WPA3-PersonalSAEDragonflyAES-CCMP

Key length rules:

  • ASCII passphrase: 8-63 characters
  • Hexadecimal: exactly 64 hex characters (32 bytes = 256 bits)
  • Most deployments use ASCII passphrases — hex PSKs are for automation scenarios

PMK derivation (WPA2):

PMK = PBKDF2(HMAC-SHA1, passphrase, SSID, 4096 iterations, 256 bits)

The PMK is derived from the passphrase + SSID. Two networks with different SSIDs but the same passphrase produce different PMKs. This means an attacker cannot pre-compute PMK rainbow tables for arbitrary networks — but they CAN pre-compute for common SSIDs like 'linksys' or 'NETGEAR' (hence PMK rainbow tables exist for popular SSIDs).

PMK derivation (WPA3): SAE derives the PMK via Dragonfly key exchange, which is a mutual authentication with an elliptic-curve-based password-to-point mapping. No offline computation possible.

left.label: WPA2-Personal (PSK)
items: Rainbow tables possible for common SSIDs

hack.memory: Personal always = shared password model. WPA2 uses PSK + 4-way handshake (crackable offline). WPA3 uses SAE (not crackable offline). UX is identical — user types a passphrase either way.
hack.practice: Calculate: at 8 chars ASCII, what is the maximum PSK keyspace? ~95^8 = 6.6 quadrillion. At 20 chars? ~95^20 = effectively uncrackable. Understand why length matters.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Exam tests Personal vs Enterprise and the PSK vs SAE distinction.

5.9.e.info:
Enterprise mode (WPA2-Enterprise or WPA3-Enterprise) uses IEEE 802.1X with a RADIUS server for per-user authentication. Each user has their own unique credentials — no shared password. This is the required security model for corporate networks.

Key differences from Personal:

  • No PSK — each user authenticates with individual credentials (username/password, certificate, smart card)
  • Requires a RADIUS authentication server (Cisco ISE, Microsoft NPS, FreeRADIUS)
  • Supports many EAP methods: EAP-TLS, PEAP, EAP-FAST, EAP-TTLS
  • Per-user session keys — compromising one user's session doesn't affect others
  • Individual accountability — syslog shows WHICH user connected
  • Centralized management — add/remove users from the RADIUS server
  • Instant revocation — disable one user without touching any AP

When to use Enterprise:

  • Corporate networks (any size)
  • Networks with more than ~20 users
  • Environments requiring audit trails
  • Regulated industries (finance, healthcare, government)

Why not use Enterprise everywhere:

  • Requires RADIUS infrastructure (server, PKI for cert-based methods, ongoing maintenance)
  • More complex client configuration (though most enterprise OSes handle this via Group Policy / MDM)
  • Overkill for a 5-person home office

Authentication Key Management (AKM) values:

  • PSK (WPA2/3-Personal)
  • 802.1X (WPA2/3-Enterprise)
  • SAE (WPA3-Personal)
  • SUITEB-192 (WPA3-Enterprise 192-bit)

hack.memory: Enterprise = 802.1X + RADIUS. Per-user credentials. Individual accountability. Revocable without touching APs. The correct answer for any network with real users.
hack.practice: Design a corporate WLAN: WPA2-Enterprise + 802.1X + Cisco ISE RADIUS + EAP-TLS with user certificates. Every user logs in with their own identity. Logs show who connected when.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam tests Personal vs Enterprise decision-making.

5.9.e.info:
The CCNA frequently presents scenarios and asks which wireless security mode to choose. A decision framework:

Choose Personal (PSK/SAE) when:

  • Home network
  • Small office with <10 users
  • No RADIUS infrastructure available or practical
  • Guest network (with isolation and throttling)
  • IoT device segment (devices that don't support 802.1X)
  • Temporary or event Wi-Fi

Choose Enterprise (802.1X) when:

  • Corporate network of any size beyond small office
  • Active Directory / identity management in place
  • RADIUS server available (ISE, NPS, FreeRADIUS)
  • Regulated industry (finance, healthcare, government)
  • Need for per-user accountability
  • Need to revoke access for individual users without affecting others

Example scenarios and answers:

  • 'Home network with 4 devices' → Personal
  • 'Corporate office, 500 employees, Active Directory exists' → Enterprise
  • 'Hotel guest network' → Personal (or OWE for open)
  • 'Hospital with HIPAA compliance needs' → Enterprise
  • 'Small law firm, 8 attorneys, wants audit trail' → Enterprise (audit trail rules out Personal)
  • 'Retail chain, 5,000 barcode scanners' → Enterprise with EAP-TLS certs (devices can't share a password at that scale)
  • 'Conference Wi-Fi, 200 attendees, 3 days' → Personal with rotating PSK, OR open with OWE

Hybrid deployments: most enterprises deploy multiple WLANs on the same infrastructure — 'Corp' (Enterprise for employees), 'Guest' (Personal with captive portal for visitors), 'IoT' (Personal with certificate for devices that can't do 802.1X). Each WLAN gets its own SSID, security policy, and VLAN mapping.

hack.memory: Decision rule: individual credentials needed? → Enterprise. Shared password fine? → Personal. Public network, no password? → OWE. Match the scenario wording to one of these three patterns.
hack.practice: Write 10 scenarios and pick Personal/Enterprise/OWE for each. Check answers. Re-read any you got wrong until the pattern is automatic.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam frequently asks scenario-based 'which mode?' questions.

5.9.f.info:
Enterprise mode implements IEEE 802.1X port-based authentication for wireless networks, using a RADIUS server as the authentication backend. Each user authenticates with individual credentials, and the RADIUS server makes the permit/deny decision based on its user database (local, LDAP/AD, certificate authority, or MFA).

The three 802.1X roles:

  1. Supplicant — the client device requesting network access (laptop, phone, tablet, IoT device). Runs 802.1X supplicant software, which is built into every modern OS (Windows, macOS, iOS, Android, Linux).
  2. Authenticator — the network device controlling access. For wireless, this is the WAP or WLC. The authenticator is a middleman — it relays authentication messages between the supplicant and the authentication server but does not make the decision itself.
  3. Authentication Server — the RADIUS server (Cisco ISE, Microsoft NPS, FreeRADIUS, Aruba ClearPass). Makes the permit/deny decision based on credentials, certificates, or other factors. Returns authorization attributes (VLAN assignment, ACL, QoS, session timeout) to the authenticator.

EAP (Extensible Authentication Protocol): the framework 802.1X uses for authentication. EAP carries authentication messages between supplicant and server via the authenticator. Common EAP methods:

  • EAP-TLS — certificate-based mutual auth. Strongest. Requires PKI. Preferred for corporate.
  • PEAP (Protected EAP) — server cert + user password in MSCHAPv2 tunnel. Easy to deploy. Common in corporate with AD.
  • EAP-FAST — Cisco, uses PAC (Protected Access Credential). Fast re-auth.
  • EAP-TTLS — similar to PEAP, more flexible inner-auth options.

Advantages over Personal mode:

  • Individual credentials per user — accountability, audit trails
  • Dynamic session keys per user (not derived from a shared PSK)
  • Centralized user management (add/remove via RADIUS)
  • Instant revocation (disable one user, others unaffected)
  • Integration with enterprise identity (AD, LDAP, SAML)
  • Dynamic authorization — RADIUS can assign VLANs, ACLs, QoS per user

hack.memory: Enterprise = 802.1X + RADIUS + INDIVIDUAL credentials. Three roles: Supplicant (client), Authenticator (AP/WLC middleman), Authentication Server (RADIUS decides). Memorize SAS.
hack.practice: Set up 802.1X in a lab: WLC as authenticator, Windows Server NPS as RADIUS, Windows 10 client as supplicant. Configure PEAP with AD auth. Verify the three-party flow in packet captures.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10 + 29. 802.1X roles are heavily tested. Know each role and which device plays it.

5.9.f.info:
The supplicant is the client device requesting network access in an 802.1X exchange. It runs software (the 802.1X supplicant) that handles the EAP conversation with the authenticator and, ultimately, the authentication server.

Supplicant responsibilities:

  • Respond to the EAPOL-Request Identity from the authenticator
  • Present credentials (username/password, client certificate, etc.) via the selected EAP method
  • Validate the authentication server's certificate (in PEAP/EAP-TLS/EAP-TTLS)
  • Derive session encryption keys after successful authentication
  • Initiate re-authentication when required (every few hours by default)

Common supplicant software:

  • Windows: built-in Wireless Auto Config Service (WLAN AutoConfig). Configured via network settings or Group Policy.
  • macOS: built-in 802.1X client. Configured via profiles (MDM) or manual network prefs.
  • iOS/iPadOS: built-in. Configured via Wi-Fi settings or MDM profiles.
  • Android: built-in per-Wi-Fi-network 802.1X settings.
  • Linux: wpa_supplicant — the reference implementation used by most Wi-Fi stacks.
  • Enterprise clients: Cisco AnyConnect NAM, Aruba ClearPass OnGuard (client-side supplicants with posture checks).

Configuration requirements (varies by EAP method):

  • EAP-TLS: client certificate, CA trust
  • PEAP: server CA trust, username/password
  • EAP-FAST: PAC (can be auto-provisioned or manually distributed)

Critical trust setting: supplicants must be configured to validate the RADIUS server's certificate. Otherwise, an attacker can run a rogue RADIUS server and harvest credentials via a fake AP (evil twin). This is the most common misconfiguration in 802.1X deployments.

hack.memory: Supplicant = CLIENT. The device asking for access. Runs the 802.1X software. Always verify the server cert — otherwise rogue RADIUS harvests credentials.
hack.practice: On a Windows 10 client, open Wi-Fi settings for an 802.1X network. Find the 'Verify certificate' option. Understand why leaving it unchecked is dangerous.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Exam: 'which 802.1X role is the client?' = supplicant.

5.9.f.info:
The authenticator is the network device that controls access to the network and relays authentication messages between the supplicant and the authentication server. In wireless deployments, the authenticator is the WAP (in autonomous mode) or the WLC (in lightweight/controller-based mode).

Authenticator responsibilities:

  • Receive EAPOL frames from the supplicant and encapsulate them as RADIUS attributes toward the server
  • Relay RADIUS responses back to the supplicant as EAPOL frames
  • Block all traffic from the supplicant except EAPOL until authentication succeeds (virtual 'unauthenticated' and 'authenticated' port states)
  • Apply RADIUS-returned authorization attributes (VLAN, ACL, QoS) to the session
  • Enforce session timeout and re-authentication policies
  • Trigger disconnection if RADIUS sends a Disconnect-Request

Critical point: the authenticator does NOT make the authentication decision. It is a middleman. The supplicant proves identity to the authentication server; the server returns Accept/Reject; the authenticator enforces the decision.

Wired vs wireless authenticator:

  • Wired (switch): each physical switch port is an 802.1X port. Commands like dot1x port-control auto enable authentication on the port.
  • Wireless (WLC/AP): 802.1X is configured per-WLAN. When a client associates, the AP/WLC initiates the EAPOL exchange.

Authenticator configuration (Cisco WLC, simplified):

  1. Define the RADIUS server under Security → AAA → Authentication (IP, shared secret, port 1812)
  2. Under WLAN → Security → Layer 2: WPA+WPA2 → WPA2 Policy ✔ → AES → Auth Key Mgmt: 802.1X
  3. Under WLAN → Security → AAA Servers: assign the RADIUS server for authentication

Now the WLC is configured as the authenticator for that WLAN.

hack.memory: Authenticator = the MIDDLEMAN. WAP/WLC for wireless, switch for wired. Relays EAP ↔ RADIUS. Does NOT decide — just enforces the server's decision. Blocks traffic until auth succeeds.
hack.practice: Configure a WLC as authenticator for a WPA2-Enterprise WLAN pointing to a RADIUS server. Understand that the WLC does not check credentials itself — it forwards them to RADIUS.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Exam: 'which 802.1X role is the WLC/AP/switch?' = authenticator.

5.9.f.info:
The authentication server is the RADIUS server that actually decides whether to permit or deny the authentication request. It holds the user database (or connects to one like AD/LDAP) and evaluates the credentials, certificate, or other authentication factors presented by the supplicant.

Common authentication servers:

  • Cisco ISE (Identity Services Engine) — full-featured, policy-driven, popular in Cisco shops
  • Microsoft NPS (Network Policy Server) — built into Windows Server, integrates natively with AD
  • FreeRADIUS — open-source, widely used, flexible
  • Aruba ClearPass — Aruba/HPE's RADIUS + policy platform
  • Cloud RADIUS — SecureW2, JumpCloud RADIUS, etc. — managed RADIUS as a service

Authentication server responsibilities:

  • Receive RADIUS Access-Request from the authenticator (containing EAP messages)
  • Engage in EAP conversation with the supplicant (via the authenticator as relay)
  • Validate credentials: check username/password against AD, validate client certificate against CA, etc.
  • Apply policy rules (allowed SSIDs, time-of-day restrictions, device posture, etc.)
  • Return Access-Accept (with authorization attributes) or Access-Reject
  • Optionally return Access-Challenge (more exchanges needed)

RADIUS protocol details:

  • UDP port 1812 for authentication (older: 1645)
  • UDP port 1813 for accounting (older: 1646)
  • Shared secret between authenticator and RADIUS server (configured on both ends)
  • RADIUS attributes (e.g., Tunnel-Private-Group-ID for VLAN assignment) returned in Access-Accept

Integration with backend identity:

  • Local users defined on the RADIUS server
  • LDAP / Active Directory integration — most common for corporate
  • Certificate CA integration for EAP-TLS
  • OAuth / SAML / MFA integration via modern RADIUS platforms

hack.memory: Auth server = RADIUS = the DECIDER. Holds or queries the user database. Returns Accept/Reject. UDP 1812 (auth) and 1813 (accounting). Shared secret with the authenticator. The real brain of 802.1X.
hack.practice: Set up FreeRADIUS or Windows NPS in a lab. Add a user. Configure a WLC to point at it. Connect a client with 802.1X. Watch RADIUS logs show Access-Request, challenges, and Access-Accept.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 10. Exam: 'which device validates credentials?' = authentication server (RADIUS).

5.10.a.info:
The Wireless LAN Controller (WLC) is the centralized management brain for a Cisco wireless network. All access points register to the WLC via CAPWAP tunnels, and the WLC is where admins create WLANs, configure security, manage radio settings, and troubleshoot client issues. The CCNA tests your ability to navigate the WLC GUI to create a WPA2-PSK WLAN.

Access methods:

  • HTTPS (Web GUI) — primary method: browse to the WLC's management IP address (e.g., https://10.0.0.2). Accept the certificate warning (WLCs ship with a self-signed cert by default). Log in with admin credentials. This is the day-to-day management interface.
  • Console cable — initial setup: out-of-the-box, a WLC has no IP configuration. Connect via serial console (RJ-45 rollover or USB) to run the initial setup wizard: hostname, management IP, gateway, admin password.
  • SSH/CLI — advanced: available for scripting and troubleshooting. Most WLC configuration uses the GUI, unlike IOS devices which rely on CLI.

Major GUI sections (top-level menu):

  • Monitor — dashboard, summary stats, AP list, client list, rogue APs
  • WLANs — create, edit, delete WLANs (this is where WLAN config lives)
  • Controller — system-level settings: interfaces, ports, NTP, mDNS, multicast
  • Wireless — AP management, RF groups, channels, power, mesh
  • Security — AAA servers, ACLs, rogue AP policies, web auth, priority order
  • Management — SNMP, syslog, HTTP access, local admin accounts, software updates
  • Commands — system commands like restart, upload/download config
  • Help — context-sensitive documentation

Navigation pattern for creating a WPA2-PSK WLAN: WLANs tab → Create New button → enter profile name, SSID, WLAN ID → configure General, Security, QoS, Advanced tabs → Apply → Enable.

hack.memory: WLC access = HTTPS to management IP (day-to-day) or console for initial setup. Main tabs: Monitor, WLANs, Controller, Wireless, Security, Management. Create WLANs in the WLANs tab.
hack.practice: Use Cisco DevNet Sandbox WLC or Packet Tracer's 3504 WLC. Navigate to each top-level tab. Know where WLANs live (WLANs tab), where AP management lives (Wireless tab), where RADIUS servers are defined (Security → AAA).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58 (WLC configuration). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam tests basic GUI navigation.

5.10.a.info:
HTTPS is the primary WLC management interface. After initial setup (via console), nearly all day-to-day configuration happens through the web GUI. The CCNA exam assumes you can navigate this GUI to create a WLAN.

Connection steps:

  1. Ensure your admin workstation has network reachability to the WLC's management IP
  2. Open a web browser and navigate to https://[management-ip]
  3. Accept the self-signed certificate warning (or install a signed certificate via Security → Advanced → Certificates)
  4. Log in with admin username/password (default admin/admin on factory-reset units — must be changed at first login)

Default ports:

  • HTTPS: TCP 443 (can be changed under Management → HTTP-HTTPS)
  • HTTP: TCP 80 (typically disabled in production — HTTPS only)

Restricting GUI access:

  • Management → HTTP-HTTPS → enable HTTPS, disable HTTP
  • Security → Access Control Lists: create an ACL permitting only management subnet IPs
  • Apply the ACL to the CPU (controller-level) to restrict both management plane access and which subnets can reach the WLC itself

Session management:

  • Management → User Sessions → HTTP/HTTPS Timeout — default 5 minutes of idle, then auto-logout
  • Multiple concurrent admin sessions allowed
  • Activity logged under Management → Logs → Message Log

Modern WLC models and GUIs:

  • Older AireOS WLC (5508, 8540, 3504): classic AireOS GUI — what CCNA typically tests
  • Newer Catalyst 9800 WLC: IOS-XE based, new GUI called 'Wireless Controller UI', more modern look
  • Core concepts (WLANs, interfaces, AP groups) are the same across both; GUI layouts differ slightly

hack.memory: HTTPS GUI = daily driver for WLC management. Port 443. Navigate to https://[mgmt-ip]. Accept cert warning. Log in. Restrict access with ACLs.
hack.practice: On a DevNet WLC sandbox, HTTPS into the GUI. Note the cert warning. Log in. Explore the Management → HTTP-HTTPS settings — disable HTTP in production.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Exam expects familiarity with HTTPS-based WLC access.

5.10.a.info:
A brand-new WLC (out of the box or after factory reset) has no IP configuration — you cannot reach it over the network until it is configured. The only way to perform initial setup is via the console port.

Initial setup process:

  1. Connect a console cable (RJ-45 rollover or USB, depending on WLC model) from your laptop to the WLC
  2. Open a terminal emulator (PuTTY, Tera Term, SecureCRT) at 9600 baud, 8-N-1
  3. Power on the WLC. The setup wizard runs automatically on first boot.
  4. Answer wizard prompts:
    • System name (hostname)
    • Admin username and password
    • Management interface IP, subnet mask, default gateway
    • Management interface VLAN (or untagged)
    • Virtual gateway IP (used by WLC internally, often 1.1.1.1)
    • Mobility/RF group name
    • 802.11a/b/g radio enable
    • Country code (for regulatory compliance — critical, affects allowed channels and power)
    • NTP server (time is critical for certificates, logs, 802.1X)
  5. After setup, the WLC reboots with the new configuration
  6. Switch to HTTPS GUI for all further management

Key wizard decisions:

  • Country code: MUST match the country of deployment. Regulatory rules differ — US, EU, JP, etc. have different allowed channels and max power levels. Setting the wrong country code can cause APs to fail to register.
  • Management IP: typically on a dedicated management VLAN
  • NTP: essential — without accurate time, certificate validation fails, 802.1X breaks, logs are useless

After setup: most admins never use the console again. HTTPS handles all ongoing work.

hack.memory: Console = initial setup ONLY. Factory WLC has no IP — must configure via console wizard. Set country code correctly or APs won't register. After setup, use HTTPS.
hack.practice: If you can get access to a real WLC or CML, factory-reset it and walk through the initial wizard. Pay attention to the country-code and NTP prompts — common real-world misconfigurations.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Exam may test 'how to initially configure a WLC?' = console port.

5.10.a.info:
WLCs support a CLI accessible via SSH (or console), but unlike IOS routers/switches, CLI is NOT the primary management interface. Most daily operations happen in the GUI. CLI is used for:

  • Advanced configuration not exposed in the GUI
  • Scripting / automation (via expect scripts or Cisco's Python SDK)
  • Troubleshooting (some debug commands are CLI-only)
  • Firmware upgrades (though this is also in GUI)
  • Backup/restore configuration

Connecting via SSH:

ssh admin@10.0.0.2

After login, you see the WLC CLI prompt — different from IOS. Commands are organized differently (e.g., show wlan summary instead of show running-config).

Common CLI commands:

  • show sysinfo — system summary
  • show wlan summary — list of WLANs
  • show ap summary — list of associated APs
  • show client summary — associated clients
  • config wlan create [id] [name] [ssid] — create a WLAN
  • save config — save config (equivalent to IOS write memory)

CLI vs IOS differences:

  • No running-config / startup-config distinction the same way — changes take effect immediately, persist via save config
  • No configuration modes (no configure terminal) — config is part of the command itself
  • Different syntax overall — CLI is a flat command structure, not hierarchical

Newer Catalyst 9800 WLC: runs IOS-XE, so CLI is more familiar to IOS admins — configure terminal, hierarchical modes, etc. Older AireOS WLCs (5508, 3504, 8540) use the flatter WLC CLI.

Exam guidance: CCNA focuses on WLC GUI, not CLI. Know that CLI exists but expect GUI-based questions.

hack.memory: WLC = GUI-first. Different from IOS. CLI exists for scripting and edge cases. Exam tests GUI navigation, not CLI syntax.
hack.practice: SSH into a DevNet WLC. Run show wlan summary, show ap summary, show client summary. Note the flat command structure — nothing like IOS.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. CLI awareness is good to have but not heavily tested.

5.10.b.info:
Creating a new WLAN on a Cisco WLC starts with three required fields. Navigate to WLANs tab → Create New → Go. The wizard prompts for:

  1. Profile Name — internal identifier used by administrators in the WLC config and reports. Appears in the WLC management interface but is NOT broadcast to clients. Example: Corporate-Employees-WLAN
  2. SSID (Service Set Identifier) — the network name that wireless clients see when scanning. This is what users select on their devices to connect. Example: CorpNet
  3. WLAN ID — a unique number (1-512) that internally identifies the WLAN on the WLC. Each WLAN must have a different ID. You can let the WLC auto-assign or pick manually.

Profile Name vs SSID distinction:

  • Profile Name is for administrators — appears only in WLC GUI, config files, logs, reports
  • SSID is for end users — broadcast in beacons, visible in 'available networks' list on clients
  • The two can be different (admin friendly name vs user-facing name)
  • In practice, many admins set them identically for simplicity — Profile Name: CorpNet and SSID: CorpNet

Initial state after creation:

  • WLAN is created but Status: Disabled by default
  • This is a safety feature — prevents accidentally broadcasting a misconfigured SSID
  • You must complete security and interface configuration before enabling
  • Enable via General tab → Status → Enabled → Apply

Configuration tabs for the new WLAN:

  • General — Status, Interface/Interface Group (VLAN mapping), SSID visibility, radio policy
  • Security — Layer 2 security (WPA2-PSK, 802.1X), Layer 3 security (web auth), AAA Servers
  • QoS — traffic prioritization
  • Policy-Mapping — advanced policy attachment
  • Advanced — session timeout, DHCP, FlexConnect, load balancing

hack.memory: Three fields to create WLAN: Profile (admin name), SSID (what users see), ID (unique 1-512). Default = DISABLED. Must configure + enable before broadcasting. Mnemonic: PSI (Profile, SSID, ID).
hack.practice: In a WLC simulator, create 3 WLANs with different profile names and SSIDs (intentionally different to prove they can diverge). Each gets a unique WLAN ID. Leave all disabled initially.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam may show the Create-WLAN screen and ask to identify the three required fields.

5.10.b.info:
Every WLAN on a WLC has three identifiers, each with a distinct purpose:

  1. Profile Name
    • Up to 32 alphanumeric characters
    • Used internally by the WLC and admin tools
    • Appears in config files, GUI tables, reports, logs
    • NOT broadcast over the air
    • Must be unique on the WLC
    • Example: Guest-WLAN-2026, Corp-Employees, IoT-Thermostats
  2. SSID
    • Up to 32 characters (per 802.11 standard)
    • Broadcast in beacon frames and probe responses
    • What end users see in their Wi-Fi list and select to connect
    • Does NOT need to be unique on the WLC — you can have two WLANs with the same SSID on different AP groups (see 5.10.g region)
    • Example: CorpNet, GuestWiFi, Company-5G
  3. WLAN ID
    • Integer 1-512 (WLC supports up to 512 WLANs total, but typically far fewer configured)
    • Internal numeric identifier
    • Used in CLI commands, debug output, RADIUS attributes
    • Must be unique on the WLC
    • WLC can auto-assign or you can pick manually

Why they are separate:

  • An admin might want a descriptive profile name ('HQ-5GHz-Engineers') while exposing a short user-friendly SSID ('Engineers')
  • Multiple WLANs with the same SSID but different profiles allow region-specific configurations (e.g., different VLANs per site while users see one SSID)
  • Numeric WLAN ID is used by automation and RADIUS — it's a stable machine identifier

hack.memory: Three identifiers. Profile + ID = INTERNAL (admin + automation). SSID = EXTERNAL (user-facing). Profile and SSID can match or differ. ID is 1-512, must be unique.
hack.practice: Create a WLAN with Profile = 'HQ-Employee-WLAN-Primary' and SSID = 'Employees'. Different values, same WLAN. Understand which each is used for.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Exam: 'what is the difference between Profile Name and SSID?' — internal label vs broadcast name.

5.10.b.info:
Critical exam point: the Profile Name and SSID can be different. They serve different purposes and do not have to match.

When they match (common):

  • Small deployments where simplicity is preferred
  • Single-site networks with no naming complexity
  • Admin convenience — one name to remember

When they differ (frequent in enterprises):

  • Multiple WLANs share the same SSID across sites (user-facing consistency)
  • Admin wants detailed internal names for reports and automation
  • Example: three WLANs at three sites all broadcast SSID CorpNet but have profile names CorpNet-HQ, CorpNet-SiteB, CorpNet-SiteC

Why the exam tests this:

  • A common exam question shows a WLAN with Profile Name 'EmployeeWLAN' and asks 'what SSID do users see?' — answer: you cannot tell from the profile name alone, you have to look at the SSID field
  • Or: 'can two WLANs have the same SSID?' — answer: yes, on the same WLC, as long as they differ in Profile Name and WLAN ID

Configuration example:

WLAN ID: 5
Profile Name: HQ-Corp-Employee-Primary-WLAN-2026
SSID: CorpNet

Users see 'CorpNet' in their Wi-Fi list. Admins see 'HQ-Corp-Employee-Primary-WLAN-2026' in the config. Both are valid.

hack.memory: Profile Name and SSID can match or differ. Profile = what admins see. SSID = what users see. Don't assume they are identical.
hack.practice: Configure a WLAN with Profile='EmployeeMain-HQ' and SSID='Corp'. Connect a client — it sees 'Corp'. Check the WLC config — it shows 'EmployeeMain-HQ'. Both are normal.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Exam trap: 'can profile name differ from SSID?' = YES.

5.10.b.info:
A newly created WLAN is Disabled by default. The WLC does not broadcast the SSID, and clients cannot connect, until an admin explicitly enables the WLAN. This is a safety feature — prevents a half-configured WLAN from accidentally going live with, say, no security or the wrong VLAN mapping.

Workflow for creating a functioning WLAN:

  1. WLANs tab → Create New → Profile / SSID / ID → Apply
  2. WLAN appears in list with Status: Disabled
  3. Click WLAN to open its config
  4. Configure General tab (Interface / Interface Group = VLAN mapping)
  5. Configure Security tab (WPA2-PSK with AES, or 802.1X, etc.)
  6. Verify Advanced tab settings (FlexConnect, session timeout, etc.)
  7. Return to General tab → check Status: Enabled → Apply
  8. WLAN is now broadcast and accepting clients

Common exam trap: 'Users report they cannot see the new SSID. What's the most likely cause?' Possible answers:

  • WLAN is still disabled (likely cause #1)
  • Broadcast SSID is unchecked (SSID is hidden)
  • AP group/FlexConnect profile doesn't include this WLAN
  • Radio policy is set to a band clients don't use

If WLAN status is Disabled, nothing works — even if every other setting is perfect.

Enabling the WLAN:

  • WLANs → select WLAN → General tab
  • Status field → set to Enabled
  • Apply button → changes take effect
  • APs start broadcasting the SSID within seconds (next beacon interval)

Verifying: after enabling, a client scan should show the SSID. If not, check: AP radio enabled, AP group includes WLAN, broadcast SSID checkbox.

hack.memory: New WLAN = DISABLED. Must flip to Enabled to broadcast. #1 'SSID not visible' troubleshooting step: check if WLAN is enabled.
hack.practice: Create a WLAN, leave disabled, scan from client — SSID not visible. Enable it, scan again — appears. The cheapest lesson in wireless troubleshooting.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Exam frequently tests 'why is SSID not visible?' — answer is WLAN not enabled.

5.10.c.info:
Configuring WPA2-PSK security on a WLC WLAN requires navigating the Security tab and setting several options in sequence. This is the canonical CCNA 5.10 task.

Step-by-step configuration (Cisco WLC AireOS GUI):

  1. WLANs tab → click the WLAN to edit → Security tab → Layer 2 subtab
  2. Layer 2 Security: dropdown → select WPA+WPA2 (this enables the WPA framework)
  3. Under WPA+WPA2 Parameters:
    • UNCHECK WPA Policy (disables legacy WPA with TKIP)
    • CHECK WPA2 Policy (enables WPA2)
    • WPA2 Encryption: CHECK AES, UNCHECK TKIP
  4. Under Authentication Key Management:
    • CHECK PSK
    • UNCHECK 802.1X (unless doing Enterprise — but this subtopic is PSK)
  5. PSK Format: ASCII or HEX (usually ASCII)
  6. Pre-Shared Key: enter the passphrase (8-63 ASCII characters recommended 15+ with complexity)
  7. Click Apply to save changes

Critical rules:

  • AES, not TKIP: TKIP is deprecated. Always select AES only. If the exam shows TKIP enabled, that's the error.
  • Disable WPA Policy: legacy WPA v1 should never be enabled on new deployments. Uncheck it.
  • Strong PSK: 15+ characters, mixed case, numbers, symbols. Short PSKs (<12 chars) are vulnerable to offline dictionary attack after a 4-way handshake capture.

What the exam tests:

  • The EXACT settings: WPA+WPA2 → WPA2 Policy → AES → PSK → passphrase
  • Spotting errors: 'TKIP is selected — change to AES'
  • Recognizing PSK format options (ASCII vs Hex)
  • Knowing PSK length requirements (8-63 ASCII)

hack.memory: WPA2-PSK config: WPA+WPA2 → WPA2 Policy ON → WPA Policy OFF → AES (not TKIP) → PSK → passphrase → Apply. Mnemonic: WWAPPA — WPA+WPA2, WPA2 ON, AES, PSK, Passphrase, Apply.
hack.practice: In a WLC simulator, walk through the full security config. Intentionally select TKIP — know that's wrong. Correct to AES. Enter a PSK, apply, enable the WLAN, connect a client with the PSK.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. The canonical 5.10 task — memorize the full GUI sequence.

5.10.c.info:
The canonical WPA2-Personal configuration combines three specific choices:

  1. Layer 2 Security: WPA+WPA2 — umbrella framework enabling WPA family support
  2. WPA2 Encryption: AES — the cipher (AES-CCMP under the hood)
  3. Authentication Key Management: PSK — pre-shared key method (Personal mode)

Together: WPA2 + AES + PSK. This is the exam's 'correct answer' pattern for any WPA2-Personal scenario.

Why these three together:

  • WPA+WPA2 framework: provides the protocol structure
  • AES encryption: strongest cipher available in WPA2 (replaces weak TKIP)
  • PSK: personal-mode authentication (vs 802.1X which is enterprise)

What NOT to select:

  • ❌ TKIP encryption — deprecated, weak, can be attacked (Beck-Tews)
  • ❌ WEP — broken since 2001
  • ❌ Dynamic WEP — still uses RC4, deprecated
  • ❌ None (open) — no encryption at all
  • ❌ WPA Policy (v1) — use WPA2 Policy only

WLC checkbox pattern:

[ ] WEP                          <-- NEVER check
[ ] Static WEP                   <-- NEVER check
[X] WPA+WPA2                     <-- check this (the framework)
    [ ] WPA Policy               <-- uncheck (deprecated)
    [X] WPA2 Policy              <-- check (enables WPA2)
        [X] AES                  <-- check (use AES)
        [ ] TKIP                 <-- uncheck (deprecated)
    Authentication Key Management:
    [X] PSK                      <-- check (Personal mode)
    [ ] 802.1X                   <-- uncheck (unless Enterprise)
    [ ] CCKM                     <-- usually unchecked
    [ ] FT                       <-- 802.11r fast transition (optional)

hack.memory: Right answer: WPA2 + AES + PSK. Wrong answer: anything with TKIP, WEP, or WPA (v1). Triangle of correctness: framework (WPA2), cipher (AES), auth method (PSK).
hack.practice: Memorize the checkbox grid. Given a WLC screenshot, identify what should be checked and what's wrong. Exam loves to show TKIP checked as the error.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Exam tests: 'Which combination is correct for WPA2-PSK?' = WPA2 + AES + PSK.

5.10.c.info:
On any CCNA exam question about WPA2 encryption configuration, TKIP is the wrong answer. Select AES instead. This is one of the most reliable 'find the error' patterns in 5.10 questions.

Why TKIP is wrong:

  • TKIP is a deprecated cipher suite, officially deprecated in 802.11-2012
  • TKIP still uses the underlying RC4 stream cipher — which has known weaknesses
  • The Beck-Tews attack (2008) can inject packets in ~15 minutes against TKIP-protected networks
  • Wi-Fi Alliance no longer certifies new products with TKIP-only support
  • Wi-Fi 6/6E (802.11ax) does NOT support TKIP at all
  • Enabling TKIP limits your WLAN to 802.11g speeds (no 802.11n/ac/ax rates) — a major performance penalty

Exam scenarios where TKIP appears:

  1. 'The WLC WLAN has WPA2 Policy enabled with TKIP checked. What's wrong?' → Answer: TKIP is deprecated, change to AES.
  2. 'Users on 802.11ax APs cannot achieve full speed. What's the likely cause?' → TKIP is enabled (forces slower rates).
  3. 'A security audit flagged the wireless network. Which setting violates modern security standards?' → TKIP enabled.

Configuration correction:

Before (wrong):
WPA2 Policy: [X]
  TKIP: [X]
  AES:  [ ]

After (correct):
WPA2 Policy: [X]
  TKIP: [ ]
  AES:  [X]

Backward-compatibility caveat: some legacy devices (old IoT, very old smartphones pre-2010) only support TKIP. In those rare cases, admins may enable TKIP + AES together on a legacy-only WLAN with strict isolation. But the CCNA exam default answer is always: use AES, not TKIP.

hack.memory: TKIP = Totally Kick It out of Production. Always wrong on exam WPA2 questions. Always pick AES. If both are enabled and you have to choose ONE, pick AES-only.
hack.practice: In WLC simulator, try enabling TKIP + AES together — some clients will negotiate TKIP, capping speed. Disable TKIP, re-test — full speed restored. The performance lesson sticks.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Exam trap: 'what's wrong with this config?' — look for TKIP enabled.

5.10.c.info:
The complete WPA2-PSK configuration sequence on a Cisco WLC — the exact click-by-click workflow tested on CCNA:

  1. Navigate: WLANs tab → find or create the WLAN → click WLAN name to open
  2. Security tab: click Security tab at the top of the WLAN config
  3. Layer 2 subtab: click Layer 2
  4. Layer 2 Security dropdown: set to WPA+WPA2
