  5. WPA+WPA2 Parameters section:
    • WPA Policy: UNCHECK
    • WPA2 Policy: CHECK
    • WPA2 Encryption: CHECK AES, UNCHECK TKIP
  6. Authentication Key Management section:
    • PSK: CHECK
    • 802.1X: UNCHECK
    • CCKM: UNCHECK (unless using Cisco fast roaming)
    • FT 802.1X: UNCHECK (unless using 802.11r fast transition)
  7. PSK Format: ASCII (default) or Hex
  8. Pre-Shared Key: enter passphrase (8-63 ASCII chars; use 15+ with complexity for security)
  9. Apply button at top-right to save
  10. General tab: verify Interface/Interface Group (VLAN), then Status → Enabled → Apply
  11. Verify: scan from a client, enter PSK, confirm association under Monitor → Clients

Common troubleshooting after config:

  • Client can't find SSID → WLAN not enabled, or Broadcast SSID unchecked
  • Client finds SSID but fails auth → wrong PSK, or TKIP-only client incompatible with AES-only WLAN
  • Client associates but no IP → VLAN/DHCP issue, check Interface mapping
  • Client gets IP but no internet → ACL, VLAN routing, default gateway

hack.memory: WPA2-PSK workflow: WLANs → Security → Layer 2 → WPA+WPA2 → WPA2 Policy + AES → PSK → passphrase → Apply → Enable WLAN. Memorize the click-path.
hack.practice: Walk through the workflow on DevNet Sandbox WLC. Time yourself — aim for <60 seconds from 'create WLAN' to 'WLAN broadcasting with WPA2-PSK'. Exam sim questions expect speed.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Exam simulation questions may ask you to replicate this exact sequence in a GUI sim.

5.10.d.info:
Every WLAN must be mapped to a WLC interface, which in turn is associated with a VLAN. This mapping determines which wired network segment wireless clients are placed on when they connect — effectively making wireless clients part of a specific VLAN, just like wired ports assigned to a VLAN.

The mapping chain:

WLAN (SSID) → WLC Dynamic Interface → VLAN ID → Wired Network Segment

Configuration workflow:

  1. First, create the dynamic interface: Controller → Interfaces → New
    • Interface Name: e.g., corp-data
    • VLAN ID: e.g., 10
    • Port Number: usually 1 or the aggregated port
    • IP address, subnet mask, gateway, DHCP server (primary and secondary)
  2. Then, map the WLAN to the interface: WLAN → General tab → Interface/Interface Group dropdown → select the dynamic interface
  3. Apply

Example enterprise design:

  • WLAN Corp → interface corp-data → VLAN 10 (employees)
  • WLAN Guest → interface guest-net → VLAN 100 (internet-only, restricted)
  • WLAN Voice → interface voice-vlan → VLAN 50 (voice QoS priority)
  • WLAN IoT → interface iot-segment → VLAN 200 (IoT devices, heavily firewalled)

Different SSIDs → different VLANs → different security zones. Same physical wireless infrastructure, multiple isolated networks.

Trunk requirement: the switch port(s) connecting the WLC to the wired network must be configured as trunk ports, with all the relevant VLANs allowed. A common failure: the WLC creates a dynamic interface in VLAN 100, but the trunk from the switch only allows VLANs 10 and 20. Clients get IPs but cannot reach the wider network.

Interface Groups: for very large deployments, Interface Groups allow a single WLAN to use multiple interfaces for load balancing — clients are distributed across several VLANs. The CCNA focuses on the single-interface case.

hack.memory: WLAN → Interface → VLAN → Wired segment. The interface is the bridge from wireless to wired. Different SSIDs → different VLANs → different security zones. Trunk port between WLC and switch MUST allow all WLAN VLANs.
hack.practice: Create two WLANs (Corp and Guest) mapped to interfaces in VLAN 10 and VLAN 100. Verify switch trunk allows both VLANs. Connect a client to Corp, check it gets a VLAN 10 IP. Connect to Guest, gets VLAN 100 IP.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam tests the WLAN→Interface→VLAN mapping concept.

5.10.d.info:
The mapping from wireless WLAN to wired VLAN is a three-layer chain:

WLAN (SSID) ──map──▶ Dynamic Interface ──tagged as──▶ VLAN ID

Layer 1: WLAN (wireless view)

  • The SSID that clients see and connect to
  • Defined under WLANs tab on WLC

Layer 2: Dynamic Interface (WLC view)

  • A logical L3 interface on the WLC
  • Has: interface name, IP address, subnet mask, gateway, DHCP server, VLAN ID, port
  • Defined under Controller → Interfaces
  • Each interface maps to one VLAN
  • The WLC has up to ~512 dynamic interfaces (one per VLAN it serves)

Layer 3: VLAN (wired view)

  • The actual 802.1Q VLAN on the wired infrastructure
  • Carries traffic between WLC and the access/distribution switches via a trunk
  • Has subnet, DHCP, routing, ACLs configured on the switch/router

How packets flow (client → internet):

  1. Client sends packet on wireless (802.11 frame, encrypted WPA2)
  2. AP receives frame, encapsulates in CAPWAP tunnel to WLC
  3. WLC decrypts, decides which dynamic interface based on the WLAN→Interface mapping
  4. WLC tags the packet with the VLAN ID of that interface
  5. WLC sends the tagged packet out the trunk port to the switch
  6. Switch forwards based on the VLAN tag
  7. Normal L2/L3 processing continues toward the destination

DHCP flow: the dynamic interface definition includes a primary/secondary DHCP server IP. When a wireless client issues a DHCPDISCOVER, the WLC relays it to the configured DHCP server. The client gets an IP in the VLAN's subnet.

Failure modes:

  • Wrong interface selected on WLAN → client lands on wrong VLAN, wrong IP
  • Trunk not allowing the VLAN → client can't reach anything outside the WLC
  • DHCP server misconfigured on dynamic interface → client gets no IP
  • VLAN not defined on the switch → packets dropped after the trunk

hack.memory: WLAN → Interface → VLAN. Three-layer chain. Interface is the bridge between wireless and wired. Each interface = one VLAN. Trunk must allow all VLANs that WLANs map to.
hack.practice: Draw the chain for your own network: WLAN name → interface name → VLAN ID → subnet → DHCP server. Trace a packet from client to internet, noting each hop.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Exam tests the mapping chain conceptually.

5.10.d.info:
To map a WLAN to a specific dynamic interface (and thus a specific VLAN), use the General tab of the WLAN configuration.

Workflow:

  1. WLANs tab → click the WLAN to edit
  2. General tab (first tab, opens by default)
  3. Find Interface/Interface Group (G) dropdown
  4. Select the desired dynamic interface (e.g., corp-data for VLAN 10)
  5. Apply

Prerequisite — creating the dynamic interface:

  1. Controller → Interfaces → New
  2. Interface Name: corp-data
  3. VLAN ID: 10
  4. Apply
  5. Edit the new interface:
    • Port Number: typically 1 (or LAG if configured)
    • IP address: e.g., 10.10.0.10 (WLC's IP in VLAN 10)
    • Netmask: 255.255.255.0
    • Gateway: 10.10.0.1
    • Primary DHCP Server: e.g., 10.10.0.5
    • Secondary DHCP Server: optional
    • Apply

Now the interface appears in the WLAN Interface dropdown and can be selected.

Default interface: WLCs come with a 'management' interface (for WLC's own management IP) and a 'virtual' interface (used internally). Neither should be used for client traffic — always create dedicated dynamic interfaces.

Interface Groups (advanced): you can group multiple dynamic interfaces and assign the group to a WLAN. The WLC round-robins client associations across the group's interfaces, distributing clients across multiple VLANs for load balancing. Useful in very large deployments. CCNA focuses on single-interface mappings.

Verifying the mapping:

  • WLANs tab → list shows Interface column
  • Controller → Interfaces → list shows which WLANs use each interface
  • Monitor → Clients → individual client record shows the VLAN assigned

hack.memory: General tab = Interface/Interface Group dropdown. That's where VLAN mapping happens. Create the interface first (Controller → Interfaces), then select it in the WLAN.
hack.practice: Create two interfaces (VLAN 10 and VLAN 100). Create two WLANs and map each to a different interface. Verify via client connection that each SSID places the client on the correct VLAN.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Exam sim may ask you to locate the Interface dropdown on the General tab.

5.10.d.info:
For the WLAN→Interface→VLAN mapping to actually work end-to-end, the switch trunk port connecting to the WLC must allow the VLANs that the dynamic interfaces are tagged with. This is a common real-world failure point.

What the switch needs:

interface GigabitEthernet1/0/24   <-- port connected to WLC
 description WLC-Uplink
 switchport mode trunk
 switchport trunk encapsulation dot1q
 switchport trunk native vlan 1
 switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,50,100,200

The allowed VLANs list must include every VLAN mapped by WLC dynamic interfaces. If VLAN 100 is used for the Guest WLAN but the trunk only allows VLANs 10 and 50, Guest clients will get IPs (from the DHCP helper) but cannot reach anything beyond the WLC — packets are dropped on the trunk.

Common exam/real-world troubleshooting scenario:

Problem: 'Users on the Guest WLAN get a 169.254.x.x APIPA address instead of a DHCP-assigned IP.'

Likely causes:

  1. Trunk port doesn't allow the Guest VLAN → DHCP discover never reaches the DHCP server
  2. DHCP scope exhausted
  3. DHCP helper-address not configured on the VLAN interface
  4. DHCP server down

Check #1 first: show interfaces trunk on the switch, verify Guest VLAN is in the 'allowed' list.

Native VLAN consideration: the WLC's trunk native VLAN (usually configured on the management interface) should match the switch's trunk native VLAN. Mismatches cause management-plane issues.

LAG (Link Aggregation): larger WLCs (5508, 8540) often have multiple uplink ports bonded into a LAG. The LAG trunk allows the same list of VLANs. Configure on both sides: switch Port-Channel + WLC LAG.

hack.memory: WLC can map WLANs to any VLAN — but if the trunk to the switch doesn't allow that VLAN, packets die. Always verify switchport trunk allowed vlan includes every WLAN's VLAN.
hack.practice: Set up two VLANs on the switch, trunk to WLC with only one in the allowed list. Create WLANs for both VLANs. Connect to each — one works, one fails. Fix the trunk allowed-list, both work.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Real-world #1 failure for new WLANs. Exam tests the trunk-VLAN requirement.

5.10.e.info:
After creating and fully configuring a WLAN (Profile/SSID/ID, Security, Interface mapping), it must be explicitly enabled before clients can see and connect. Newly created WLANs are Disabled by default — a safety feature preventing a half-configured WLAN from being accidentally broadcast.

Enabling the WLAN:

  1. WLANs tab → click the WLAN to edit
  2. General tab
  3. Status field → select Enabled
  4. Click Apply at the top-right
  5. Change takes effect within a few seconds — APs begin broadcasting the SSID in beacons

Verification checklist after enabling:

  1. WLAN list shows Enabled: WLANs tab → status column shows green 'Enabled'
  2. APs are broadcasting the SSID: Wireless tab → associated APs should show the WLAN in their config. Some APs may need a few seconds to pick up the new WLAN.
  3. Clients can discover the SSID: scan for Wi-Fi from a client device — the SSID should appear in the available networks list.
  4. Clients can authenticate: enter the PSK on a client. Verify it connects and receives an IP via DHCP.
  5. Traffic flows correctly: ping, DNS lookup, browse — verify the client is on the correct VLAN and can reach expected resources.

Troubleshooting: 'Users report they cannot see the new SSID.'

  1. Is the WLAN Status set to Enabled? (check first — most common issue)
  2. Is 'Broadcast SSID' checked in General tab? (if unchecked, SSID is hidden — clients must know the name)
  3. Are the APs in the right AP Group with this WLAN included?
  4. Is the radio (2.4GHz / 5GHz) enabled on the APs?
  5. Are the APs actually up and associated with the WLC?

For troubleshooting step 1 (WLAN not enabled) — 90% of 'SSID invisible' scenarios. Always check first.

hack.memory: Creating a WLAN does NOT enable it. MUST flip Status to Enabled. General tab → Status: Enabled → Apply. #1 troubleshooting step for 'SSID not visible' = check if WLAN is enabled.
hack.practice: Lab: create WLAN, configure everything, leave Disabled. Scan from client — SSID missing. Enable it — SSID appears. Connect. This sequence is the cheapest way to burn the lesson in.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Easy exam point: 'what's the final step to make a new SSID visible?' = enable the WLAN.

5.10.e.info:
When a WLAN's Status is Disabled, the WLC does NOT instruct APs to broadcast the SSID. The WLAN exists in the configuration, but from a client's perspective, the network does not exist.

What happens with a disabled WLAN:

  • APs do not include the SSID in beacon frames
  • APs do not respond to probe requests for the SSID
  • Clients scanning for Wi-Fi do NOT see the SSID in their list
  • Any client attempting to associate (if they somehow know the SSID) is rejected

Why default is Disabled:

  • Safety — prevents accidentally broadcasting a WLAN before security is properly configured
  • Without security, a briefly-enabled WLAN might accept open connections and expose the network
  • Without correct VLAN mapping, clients could land on the wrong network and cause routing issues

Common error: forgetting to enable

Admin flow goes:

  1. Create WLAN → ✓
  2. Configure Security (WPA2-PSK with AES) → ✓
  3. Map to Interface/VLAN → ✓
  4. Click Apply → ✓
  5. Forget to enable → ✗

Admin tells users 'WLAN is ready.' Users can't see it. Admin spends 10 minutes troubleshooting radios, APs, trunks before realizing the WLAN is still Disabled.

Mass-enable/disable: no single toggle for 'enable all WLANs' — each must be enabled individually (though automation/scripting can do this). Some WLC versions allow multi-select in the WLAN list to enable/disable multiple at once.

hack.memory: Disabled WLAN = INVISIBLE. Clients can't see, can't connect. Created ≠ Enabled. Must explicitly flip to Enabled after configuration.
hack.practice: Create a WLAN, leave disabled. Scan from phone/laptop — SSID not in list even though WLAN exists on WLC. Enable — SSID appears. Done.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Exam: 'SSID not visible — what to check first?' = WLAN Status enabled.

5.10.e.info:
The exact steps to enable a WLAN on a Cisco WLC:

  1. WLANs tab: click at the top of the WLC GUI
  2. Select the WLAN: click the WLAN ID or Profile Name in the list
  3. General tab: opens by default when you edit a WLAN
  4. Status checkbox: find the 'Status' row — check the Enabled checkbox
  5. Apply button: top-right of the page, click to save the change
  6. Confirmation message appears: 'WLAN created' or 'WLAN updated successfully'
  7. Return to WLANs list — the WLAN now shows Status: Enabled

Alternative via CLI:

(Cisco Controller) > config wlan enable 1    <-- enables WLAN ID 1

Or for all WLANs:

(Cisco Controller) > config wlan enable all

Effect in seconds: APs receive the config update from the WLC via CAPWAP within a few seconds. The next beacon interval (default ~100ms) includes the new SSID. Clients scanning within a few hundred milliseconds see the SSID appear in their list.

Logging: enabling a WLAN generates a syslog message:

%AAA-3-AUTH_USER_SWITCH: user admin from 10.10.0.50 enabled WLAN '1' (name 'CorpNet')

This provides an audit trail — useful for compliance and troubleshooting.

Immediate post-enable check: from a nearby client, scan for Wi-Fi. The SSID should appear within seconds. If it doesn't:

  • Confirm WLAN is actually enabled (re-check General tab)
  • Confirm 'Broadcast SSID' is checked
  • Confirm the AP group includes this WLAN
  • Confirm APs are up and associated with the WLC

hack.memory: WLANs → WLAN → General → Status: Enabled → Apply. Five clicks. Effect in seconds. Verify by scanning from a client.
hack.practice: Time yourself: can you go from 'WLAN disabled' to 'SSID visible on phone' in under 20 seconds? Practice until fluid — exam sim questions reward speed.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Exam sim may ask you to enable a WLAN in a GUI task.

5.10.e.info:
The single most common 'why doesn't this work?' scenario in WLC WLAN deployment: admin created and configured the WLAN perfectly, but forgot to flip Status to Enabled. On the CCNA exam, whenever you see a scenario where a new SSID is invisible and all other settings appear correct, check WLAN status first.

Why this trap is so common:

  • Admins focus on the 'hard' parts — security, VLAN mapping, AP grouping
  • The 'easy' part — flip a checkbox to Enabled — is overlooked
  • The GUI doesn't strongly prompt 'remember to enable' after saving security/interface config
  • The WLAN appears in the list, which feels like 'done' — but disabled WLANs are just drafts

Exam-style troubleshooting flow:

Question: 'An administrator created a new WLAN with profile name Guest-2026 and SSID Guest. The security is WPA2-PSK with AES and a strong password. The WLAN is mapped to VLAN 100 and the trunk allows VLAN 100. Users report they cannot see the Guest SSID. What is the most likely cause?'

Answer: The WLAN is not enabled. Check WLAN → General tab → Status.

Troubleshooting checklist (in order):

  1. Is the WLAN Status set to Enabled? ← #1 check
  2. Is 'Broadcast SSID' checkbox on in General tab?
  3. Are the APs associated with the WLC? (Wireless tab)
  4. Is the AP group (if using AP groups) configured with this WLAN?
  5. Is the radio band enabled on the APs? (2.4GHz and/or 5GHz)
  6. Is the client within range of an AP broadcasting this WLAN?

Real-world anecdote: countless network engineers have spent hours troubleshooting a new wireless deployment before realizing the WLAN was never enabled. Put 'enable the WLAN' on a physical post-it note until the habit is automatic.

hack.memory: #1 'new SSID invisible' cause: WLAN never enabled. Before troubleshooting anything fancy — radios, APs, trunks — CHECK IF IT'S ENABLED. Saves hours of wasted effort.
hack.practice: Every time you create a WLAN in a lab, deliberately skip enabling. Scan — SSID missing. Enable — SSID present. Wire the mental habit: 'the last step is always Enable.'
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Highest-value easy-point on the CCNA exam.
